4.3. Extracellular matrix deposition and remodelling: a focus on
collagen
In line with the general higher speed of regeneration, appearance of
an organised ﬁbrous extracellular matrix (ECM) occurs earlier in brittle
stars than in starﬁshes. In both echinoderm models nonﬁbrillar
collagen-like molecules are ﬁrstly deposited. However, it is at the end
of the repair phase that collagen ﬁbrils and ﬁbril bundles become
visible (Fig. S2; Ben Khadra et al., 2015a, 2015b, 2017).
To better deﬁne collagen production/deposition, the gene expres-
sion of a key collagen biosynthetic enzyme (prolyl-4-hydroxylase; p4h)
was investigated. Till now, few studies have focused on the expression
of p4h in invertebrates (Veijola et al., 1994; Abrams and Andrew,
2012) and in particular in marine invertebrates (Pozzolini et al., 2015).
In this context, this study represents a pioneering work. In brittle stars
this gene is not apparently expressed in the ﬁrst phase after injury (it
becomes visible only at advanced stages in the coelomic epithelium),
whereas in starﬁshes it is localised in the regenerating epidermis at the
middle/late repair phase, supporting a role of this tissue in early
collagen biosynthesis/deposition. For A. ﬁliformis the apparent incon-
gruences between the absence of Aﬁ-p4h expression till the onset of the
regenerative phase and the microscopic detection of collagen from the
end of the repair phase need to be further investigated through
quantitative PCR (also at earlier regenerative stages).
Noteworthy, in both echinoderms ECM deposition starts later than in
mammals (Clore et al., 1979): indeed, in the latter new collagen appears
at the very beginning of the repair phase (from about 10 h after injury).
A reticular and disorganised ﬁbrillar network of collagen type III is ﬁrstly
deposited and then replaced by thick, dense and parallel ﬁbres of collagen
type I that are constantly remodelled (Xue and Jackson, 2015).
Diﬀerently, in echinoderms collagen deposition begins only at the end
of the repair phase and it initially occurs as non-ﬁbrillar loose ECM,
possibly providing a more “dynamic and plastic” environment for tissue
regeneration.
Moreover, as already
suggested
for
sea cucumbers
(Quiñones et al., 2002) and contrary to mammals (Bock and Mrowietz,
2002; Rahban and Garner, 2003), in both brittle stars and starﬁshes no
ﬁbrotic scar is normally detected.
4.4. Conclusions
In this study the brittle star Amphiura ﬁliformis and the starﬁsh
Echinaster sepositus were used as models to describe and compare the
repair phase phenomena after arm amputation within echinoderms as
well as with mammals’ healing events after wound. The main simila-
rities and diﬀerences between them are summarised in Table 1 and
Fig. 6.
Taken together, our results show that:
• both echinoderm models display similar haemostasis, wound con-
traction and re-epithelialisation phenomena and, in comparison to
mammals, they are overall more eﬃcient during the emergency
reaction after injury in terms of timing and eﬃcacy;
• the regenerating epidermis of echinoderms is apparently a highly
active and multi-functional tissue, involved in both inﬂammatory/
immune response (phagocytosis), plus in collagen biosynthesis;
• the extracellular matrix (ECM) ﬁbrillar organisation after injury is
comparable in the two echinoderm models and it is delayed and less
conspicuous than in mammals. Moreover, over-deposition of col-
lagen (ﬁbrosis) is never detectable. Overall, the temporary loose
conﬁguration of the ECM is likely to be more “plastic” than the

collagenous scar of mammals, therefore possibly facilitating the
subsequent regenerative process, as suggested for sea cucumbers
(Quiñones et al., 2002).
It is important to point out that re-epithelialisation, inﬂammatory/
immune system-related genes and ECM ﬁbrillar organisation/deposi-
tion during brittle star and starﬁsh repair phases were here deeply
described. Furthermore, interesting diﬀerences and similarities in
repair events and timing within echinoderms and between echino-
derms and mammals were highlighted. The comparison between
animals able or unable to regenerate after injury suggests that
regenerative abilities are mechanistically diverse from the very ﬁrst
repair events. These diﬀerences, contrary to what is assumed, are not
just diﬀerences in the subsequent re-growth capacities. In the future,
perturbation tests aimed to impair/block re-epithelialisation, immune
response or ECM deposition should be performed to test the hypothesis
that speciﬁc repair events are strictly necessary to permit an eﬃcient
regenerative process. Moreover, our ﬁndings show that echinoderms,
and starﬁshes especially, can be considered valid alternative models to
study wound healing and regeneration in light of human health future
applications (Gurtner et al., 2008).

===== Ferretti2003_DevDyn_SCregen+Phylogenesis =====
REVIEWS
A PEER REVIEWED FORUM
Changes in Spinal Cord Regenerative Ability
Through Phylogenesis and Development: Lessons
to Be Learnt
Patrizia Ferretti,* Fang Zhang, and Paul O’Neill
Lower vertebrates, such as ﬁsh and amphibians, and developing higher vertebrates can regenerate complex body
structures, including signiﬁcant portions of their central nervous system. It is still poorly understood why this potential
is lost with evolution and development and becomes very limited in adult mammals. In this review, we will discuss the
current knowledge on the cellular and molecular changes after spinal cord injury in adult tailed amphibians, where
regeneration does take place, and in developing chick and mammalian embryos at different developmental stages.
We will focus on the recruitment of progenitor cells to repair the damage and discuss possible roles of changes in
early response to injury, such as cell death by apoptosis, and of myelin-associated proteins, such as Nogo, in the
transition between regeneration-competent and regeneration-incompetent stages of development. A better
understanding of the mechanisms underlying spontaneous regeneration of the spinal cord in vivo in amphibians and
in the chick embryo will help to devise strategies for restoring function to damaged or diseased nervous tissues in
mammals. Developmental Dynamics 226:245–256, 2003. © 2003 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key words: apoptosis; cavitation; chick; ependyma; FGF; haemorrhage; myelin; opossum; spinal cord; regeneration; urodele
amphibia
Received 20 August 2002; Accepted 28 October 2002
INTRODUCTION
The most common outcome of trau-
matic injuries to spinal cord and
brain in adult mammals, including
humans, is signiﬁcant tissue loss. This
loss leads to crippling conditions
since, once neural tissue is lost, it
cannot be replaced because of the
apparently
limited
ability
of
the
mammalian central nervous system
(CNS) to repair itself. Initial disruption
of tissue architecture and cellular ly-
sis within the spinal cord is directly
caused by the injury itself (Velardo
et al., 2000). The extent of the pri-
mary injury varies with the degree
and type of trauma and triggers sev-
eral other secondary tissue damage
pathways (Fig. 1). These processes
can result in spread of the injury site
to distal locations and greatly in-
crease the numbers of cells lost and
extent of tissue damage in addition
to affecting long-distance neurite
extension from surviving neurones.
Although the precise mechanisms
leading to expansion of primary inju-
ries are not fully understood, several
factors are known to be implicated
in secondary tissue damage, and
some of them are summarised in Fig-
ure 1. Therefore, key requirements
for successful spinal cord regenera-
tion are the presence of an ade-
quate number of neurons and glia,
either as the result of de novo pro-
duction from neural stem cells or of
signiﬁcant cell survival after injury,
and the ability of these neurones to
grow axons that can successfully re-
innervate their targets.
Much work has focused on at-
tempts to induce regeneration post-
natally in mammalian CNS and on
the
developmental
mechanisms
leading to the formation of the CNS
on the assumption that reactivation
of such events after injury may over-
Developmental Biology Unit, Institute of Child Health, UCL, London, United Kingdom
Grant sponsor: The Wellcome Trust; Grant number: 059654; The Child Research Appeal Trust; Grant number: 95-DB-23; Grant number:
97-DB-09.
*Correspondence to: Patrizia Ferretti, Developmental Biology Unit, Institute of Child Health, 30 Guilford Street, London WC1N 1EH, UK.
E-mail: ferretti@ich.ucl.ac.uk
DOI 10.1002/dvdy.10226
DEVELOPMENTAL DYNAMICS 226:245–256, 2003
© 2003 Wiley-Liss, Inc.

come the inability to regenerate.
Less attention, however, has been
paid to systems that can spontane-
ously regenerate the nervous system
and recover signiﬁcant function, al-
though it can be argued that once
the rules governing regeneration are
understood, it may be possible to
apply them to induce regeneration
in species that normally do not re-
generate.
Regeneration of the adult nervous
system occurs efﬁciently in a limited
number of vertebrates, such as ﬁsh
and amphibians, and even within
these groups signiﬁcant differences
in
regenerative
capability
have
been reported (Goss, 1969; Clarke
and Ferretti, 1998). For example,
among amphibians, only the tailed
ones,
urodeles
(e.g.,
newts
and
salamanders), regenerate their spi-
nal cord, retina, and even part of
the telencephalon as adults. In con-
trast, anurans (tailless amphibians
such as frogs and Xenopus) can re-
generate neural structures only as
larvae
and
such
capability
de-
creases with the progression of de-
velopment
(Holder
and
Clarke,
1988; Filoni, 1992). A high regenera-
tive ability during development is
not unique to anurans, and it has
been well documented in higher
vertebrates, such as birds and mar-
supials, a mammal subclass, as their
embryos are easily accessible for
manipulation
and
analysis
unlike
those from species where the entire
gestation is intrauterine. There are
also indications that the spinal cord
may regenerate in rodent embryos,
although this does not seem to be
the case in sheep (De Beer et al.,
1992; Meuli-Simmen et al., 1996; Yin-
gling et al., 1999).
Changes in regenerative ability
are most likely due to combinations
of several factors, such as cellular
and molecular changes acquired
during evolution/development, en-
vironmental
difference
between
embryonic and adult CNS, and a
decrease in the number of neural
progenitors, or the ability to recruit
them in vivo in the adult CNS of
higher
vertebrates.
Recent
evi-
dence of the existence of multipo-
tent
neural
progenitors
in
adult
mammalian
CNS
and
of
much
higher neural cell plasticity, at least
in vitro, than previously believed
(Reynolds and Weiss, 1992; Mors-
head et al., 1994; Vescovi et al.,
1999; Gage, 2000; Alvarez-Buylla et
al., 2002; Song et al., 2002) makes
the question “what makes regener-
ation possible in adult urodeles and
in developing higher vertebrates?”
a crucial one to be answered.
It is unthinkable to even attempt
to provide a comprehensive over-
view of spinal cord regeneration in a
relatively limited space, given the
several
issues
involved
and
their
complexity, and the abundance of
contributions to the ﬁeld. Therefore,
here we will focus on systems that
can regenerate their spinal cords
and discuss mainly the replenish-
ment and survival of neural cells at
the injury site, as in the absence of
such a population no axon would
regrow, and the role that changes in
early response to injury and myelina-
tion may have in the transition from
regeneration-competent to -incom-
petent stages of development in
higher vertebrates. In particular, we
Fig. 1.
Effects of secondary injury mechanisms on spinal cord pathology. The extent of
the initial mechanical injury is greatly extended over time by secondary injury processes.

after

note the large gap between the two spinal

continuity (arrow) of the spinal cord (SC)
has been re-established. Scale bars  250

vimentin, glial ﬁbrillary acidic protein (GFAP), and cytokeratins 8 and 18 (CK 8 and 18) in
normal urodele ependymoglial cells and regenerating ependymal tube compared with
the developing neural tube in higher vertebrates.

will discuss regeneration of the adult
amphibian spinal cord and of devel-
oping chick and opossum spinal
cords.
ADULT URODELE AMPHIBIANS
CAN REGENERATE THE SPINAL
CORD
Regeneration of the spinal cord can
occur both after cord transection
and tail amputation. As illustrated in
Figure 2, after spinal cord transec-
tion, there is retraction of the cord
stumps with consequent formation
of a gap that will be rapidly reﬁlled
by cells migrating from both cut sur-
faces. It has been suggested that
during regeneration in this model,
there is an epithelia to mesenchy-
mal transition to allow such migra-
tion (O’Hara et al., 1992; Chernoff,
1996). Once the two stumps have
rejoined, the cells that will regener-
ate the cord revert to an epithelial
phenotype. In contrast, after tail am-
putation, the cut end of the cord is
rapidly sealed to form a terminal
vesicle and there is no evidence of
an epithelia to mesenchymal transi-
tion. In both cases, however, pheno-
typical changes in the cells lining the
central canal (Fig. 3), that appear to
be the major contributors to the re-
generate, are observed (Chernoff et
al.,
1998;
Walder,
2002).
These
changes include up-regulation of
the cytoskeletal protein nestin that is
considered a neural progenitor cell
marker (Zhang, Walder, and Ferretti,
unpublished observations). This ﬁnd-
ing suggests that these cells must un-
dergo an “activation” process to be
enrolled for regeneration.
After tail amputation, an ependy-
mal tube, resembling a developing
neural tube, forms from cells that mi-
grate from the stump toward the ter-
minal vesicle, and elongates by cell
proliferation. Proliferating cells are in-
terspersed along the entire length of
the ependymal tube, with the ex-
ception
of
the
terminal
vesicle
where mitotic ﬁgures are less fre-
quently
observed
(Holtzer,
1956;
Egar and Singer, 1972; Iten and Bry-
ant, 1976; Nordlander and Singer,
1978; Zhang et al., 2000). Nonethe-
less, after tail amputation, differenti-
ation of the ependymal tube occurs
in a proximal-to-distal direction and
the regenerating spinal cord closer
to the level of amputation is always
more differentiated than its most
caudal tip. Differentiation into neu-
rons and glia, axonal growth, and
establishment of appropriate con-
nection with their targets is usually
complete within a few weeks (Iten
and Bryant, 1976; Clarke et al., 1988;
Davis et al., 1989; Thouveny et al.,
1991). The fact that several develop-
mentally regulated molecules are
either maintained in the adult spinal
cord (e.g., wnt-10) or up-regulated
in response to injury (e.g., the embry-
onic isoform of NCAM, PSA-NCAM)
suggests that much of the process of
spinal cord formation is similarly con-
trolled during spinal cord develop-
ment and regeneration (Chernoff,
1996; Clarke and Ferretti, 1998).
Progenitor Cells for
Regeneration
A key issue in regeneration concerns
the origin of the neural progenitors
that will give rise to the regenerate. It
is still an open question whether the
new cord originates from stem cells
or by means of a process of dedif-
ferentiation. This process is deﬁned
as the ability of a differentiated cell
to regress to a less-mature state, re-
enter the cell cycle, and subse-
quently redifferentiate to replenish a
missing
cell
population.
Whereas
much evidence for dedifferentiation
is now available in the case of the
regenerating
amphibian
limb
(Brockes, 1998; Velloso et al., 2001),
the issue of dedifferentiation versus
stem cells in the case of spinal cord
regeneration is more ambiguous. It
has been recently suggested that
the ependymal cells of the newt
spinal cord undergo a process of
dedifferentiation on the basis that
no signiﬁcant loss of regenerative
ability seems to occur in the cord
after multiple tail amputations (Mar-
gotta et al., 2002). These authors ar-
gue that, if regeneration of the adult
amphibian cord were due to a “re-
serve” cell population, this would
eventually become depleted. “Re-
serve” cells would presumably be
equivalent to pluripotent progenitors
found in mammals, such as the mul-
tipotent mesenchymal cells of the
bone marrow that do not seem to
have inﬁnite self-renewing capability
and decrease in number with age
(Banﬁet al., 2000; Huibregtse et al.,
2000). A role for “reserve” cells, how-
ever, has never been demonstrated
in the context of organ regeneration
in amphibians. An alternative expla-
nation to the occurrence of dedif-
ferentiation after spinal injury could
be that the ependyma harbours a
self-renewing stem cell population.
Cell proliferation, although rare, is in-
deed observed in the ependyma of
uninjured spinal cords (Holder and
Clarke, 1988; Holder et al., 1991;
Zhang et al., 2000). However, the
speed at which an ependymal tube
starts to form, together with the rela-
tive length of the cell cycle in urode-
les, is more suggestive of massive cell
recruitment than of recruitment of a
minute cell population. As already
mentioned above, changes in the
expression
of
neural
progenitor
markers in response to tail amputa-
tion suggest that the cells contribut-
ing to the regenerate are at least
somehow “activated” in order to
behave as neural progenitors and
regenerate the cord, and that this
process is triggered by the injury.
Whether the “activation” of spinal
cord cells for regeneration is equiv-
alent to a dedifferentiation process
is not clear, and might in the end be
a matter of deﬁnition.
The mature urodele spinal cord is
fundamentally similar to that of other
vertebrates, apart from a structural
characteristic of the cells lining the
ependymal canal. In adult urodeles,
these cells maintain contact with
the pia mater, therefore, resembling
radial glia (Schonbach, 1969; Holder
et al., 1990). In higher vertebrates,
radial glia disappears with develop-
ment and is not present in the adult
spinal cord. Therefore, the cells lining
the
ependymal
canal
in
adult
urodeles are also called ependymo-
glial cells, and in this review, we will
use this deﬁnition to distinguish them
from the radial glia normally present
during development.
The ependymal tube that forms
after tail amputation in urodeles is
believed
to
originate
from
the
ependymoglial
cells
of
the
cord
stump (Egar et al., 1970; Nordlander
and Singer, 1978), although it is not
OCCURRENCE OF SPINAL CORD REGENERATION 247

known whether all of these cells or
only a subset of them contribute to
spinal
cord
regeneration.
The
ependymoglial cells are likely to be
a heterogeneous cell population on
the basis of morphologic and mo-
lecular differences (Holder et al.,
1990; Tomizawa et al., 2000), and it is
not known whether they are all mul-
tipotent or some of them are al-
ready committed to different lin-
eages.
Although these issues have yet to
be further elucidated, once ependy-
moglial
cells
have
formed
the
ependymal tube, they can give rise
to the different cell types of the spi-
nal cord. Labelling with bromode-
oxyuridine (BrdU) of these cell popu-
lations both at early (Fig. 4) and at
later stages (Benraiss et al., 1999)
suggests that neurons originate from
the ependymal tube. Double-stain-
ing experiments have not been fully
conclusive, however, because de-
tection of early markers of neuronal
differentiation is not compatible with
the
protocol
for
BrdU
detection.
Therefore, further experiments using
additional neuronal markers and ex-

grated in the mantle zone. B: Double stain-
ing for BrdU (green) and the neuronal
marker NeuN (red). Some areas of possible
overlap (arrow) are observed, but BrdU-la-
belled neuroblasts might not be expressing
signiﬁcant levels of NeuN, which is most
commonly found in more mature neu-
rones. Scale bars  50 m in A,B.
Fig. 5.

stages permissive E11 (embryonic day 11) and nonpermissive (E15) for regeneration.

longitudinal sections of chick spinal cords

injury of E11 spinal cord; localised damage

days after injury of E11spinal cord; the mor-

C,D.
248 FERRETTI ET AL.

amining more time points will have
to be carried out to demonstrate
deﬁnitely that the majority of neu-
rons
originate
from
activated
ependymoglia. Strikingly, in addition
to this source of neurons, the new
cord contains neurons that have
translocated from the spinal cord
stump into the regenerate, as re-
cently shown by monitoring the be-
haviour in response to tail amputa-
tion of retrogradely labelled neurons
(Zhang et al., 2002b). This contribu-
tion of cells to the regenerate in
adult newts is consistent also with
the observation that there is migra-
tion of some periventricular grey
matter with the ependymal cells af-
ter ablation of cord segments in lar-
val Xenopus (Filoni et al., 1984; Filoni,
1992).
The key role played by ependy-
moglial cells in urodele spinal cord
regeneration is of much interest, par-
ticularly given important recent work
that has changed the way we think
of radial glia. It is now apparent that
the radial glia does not only play a
role in supporting and guiding neu-
ronal migration before terminal dif-
ferentiation in the astrocytic lineage
but that it can give rise to both as-
trocytes and neurons in chick and
rodent developing brains (Gray and
Sanes, 1992; Miyata et al., 2001;
Noctor et al., 2001; Alvarez-Buylla et
al., 2002). Consistent with this multi-
potentiality is that most progenitor
cells in the ventricular zone have a
radial glia morphology (Noctor et
al.,
2002).
Expression
of
pax6
in
mouse radial glia progenitors but not
in other neural progenitors appears
to be a determinant of neurogenic
fate, and it would be interesting to
assess whether pax6 is either ex-
pressed by ependymoglial cells, or
induced in these cells in response to
injury (Heins et al., 2002). It is not
known at present whether in mam-
mals spinal cord radial glia behave
in a manner similar to that in the
brain, but in amphibians and ﬁsh this
would appear to be the case during
regeneration.
It
has
yet
to
be
established
whether,
in
urodeles,
radial
glia
plays a more extensive contribution
to spinal cord development than in
mammals, or its role is mainly in gen-
eration of neurones throughout the
life of the animal (Holder et al.,
1991). Surprisingly, whereas it has
been shown that neural stem cells
from mammals, including humans,
can be cultured long-term, it has not
been possible so far to achieve a
similar long-term success with cultur-
ing multipotent spinal cord cells from
urodeles.
Neither
Chernoff
et
al.
(1998) nor we (Walder, 2002) have
been able to grow cells from normal
or regenerating urodele spinal cord
for more than a few weeks. This ﬁnd-
ing may be due either to the fact
that optimal culture conditions have
yet to be found or to intrinsic prop-
erties of this cell population. The only
long-term
cultures
obtained
from
urodele spinal cord display a glia
phenotype and have never been
seen to differentiate into neurones
either in vitro or when grafted into
the regenerating tail (Benraiss et al.,
1996, 1997). Although no radial glia is
retained in the mature mammalian
cord, the presence of a small popu-
lation of slowly proliferating ependy-
mal cells, suggested to be neural
stem cells because of their differen-
tiation potential in vitro, has been
reported (Johansson et al., 1999). It
would
be
interesting
to
assess
whether these cells transiently ac-
quire a radial glia-like phenotype
during their process of differentiation
in vitro, and whether this phenotype
is not induced in vivo in response to
injury. If the latter is the case, possibly
because of the nonpermissive envi-
ronment of the adult spinal cord, it
may at least in part explain why ro-
dent ependymal cells display little
plasticity in vivo.
Spinal Ganglia Regeneration
After resection of a few spinal seg-
ments, as well as after tail amputa-
tion, not only the spinal cord but also
the associated ganglia regenerate.
These structures, like the Schwann
cells
myelinating
the
peripheral
nerves and melanocytes, develop
from the neural crest. At present, the
origin of these cells in the regenerat-
ing cord is not clear. It is evident,
however, that simple ablation of a
spinal ganglion will not trigger regen-
eration of this structure. In contrast, if
at least part of the spinal cord is ab-
lated together with the ganglion, re-
generation of all of the missing struc-
tures will ensue (Bernardini et al.,
1992; Filoni et al., 1995). Under these
circumstances, an ependymoglial
response
with
formation
of
an
ependymal tube occurs, and this
occurrence is believed to be funda-
mental to ganglion regeneration.
It is not understood whether re-
generation of the peripheral nervous
system is a close recapitulation of
development, and a neural crest–
like structure forms within the dorsal
ependymal tube during spinal cord
regeneration in adult animals. This
possibility seemed unlikely on the
basis of cellular and molecular anal-
ysis, suggesting that dorsal root gan-
glia precursor cells migrate from the
ventral aspect of the regenerating
cord (Arsanto et al., 1992; Nicolas et
al., 1996). However, it has been re-
cently proposed that the discontinu-
ity of the basement membrane ob-
served in the dorsal region of the
terminal vesicle is indicative of the
existence of neural crest-like cells in
the dorsal region that emigrate as
during development (Benraiss et al.,
1997). In whole-mount preparations
of regenerating tails, we have also
observed cells apparently migrating
from the most distal part of the
ependymal tube. It will be techni-
cally challenging to label and mon-
itor presumptive neural crest–like cell
populations, but a better under-
standing of the source of cells for
ganglion
regeneration
and
the
mechanism controlling it would help
to tackle the issue of sensory regen-
eration in higher vertebrates.
Signals and Molecules for
Regeneration
Little is known of the molecular event
required to evoke a regenerative re-
sponse. Modulation of extracellular
matrix and adhesion molecule ex-
pression (Arsanto et al., 1990; Caubit
et al., 1993, 1994), metalloprotein-
ases, transcription factors (Nicolas et
al., 1996, 1999), and signalling mole-
cules (Caubit et al., 1997a, b) have
all been suggested as contributing
factors on the basis of studies in
other species or other regenerating
systems (Chernoff, 1996). Recently,
we have examined the role of FGF
(ﬁbroblast growth factor) signaling in
OCCURRENCE OF SPINAL CORD REGENERATION 249

spinal cord regeneration because of
the important function of FGF2 dur-
ing
neural
development
and
in
maintaining mammalian neural pro-
genitor cells cycling in vitro (Gritti et
al., 1996; Qian et al., 1997; Shihabud-
din et al., 1997; Alvarez et al., 1998;
Palmer et al., 1999; Tropepe et al.,
1999). In addition, FGF2 is up-regu-
lated after spinal trauma in mam-
mals (Follesa et al., 1994; Mocchetti
et al., 1996) and endogenous FGF2
has been shown to improve neuro-
nal survival and functional recovery
in injured spinal cord (Teng et al.,
1998, 1999; Rabchevsky et al., 1999,
2000). The mechanism of action pos-
sibly involves inhibition of apoptosis
as FGF2 has been shown to prevent
caspase-3
activation
in
neuronal
cells in vitro (Miho et al., 1999).
In the urodele amphibian Pleu-
rodeles waltl, there is an early in-
crease in the levels of ﬁbroblast
growth factor 2 (FGF2) in the spinal
cord after tail amputation (Zhang et
al., 2000). Expression of FGF2 paral-
lels that of markers of cell prolifera-
tion in the regenerating cord. This
ﬁnding, together with an increase in
cell proliferation within the ependy-
mal tube observed after application
of FGF-coated beads in vivo, sug-
gests that a role of FGF2 is to sustain
proliferation of neural progenitors
during regeneration. This suggestion
is consistent with a FGF2 requirement
for proliferation of mammalian neu-
ral progenitor cells in several in vitro
systems, and the reduction in neu-
rone number observed in cortex and
hippocampus
of
Fgf2
knockout
mice (Ortega et al., 1998; Raballo et
al., 2000; Cheng et al., 2002; Korada
et al., 2002). The importance of FGF
signalling is further supported by ev-
idence that FGF receptors (FGFRs)
are differentially regulated during
spinal cord regeneration in adult
urodeles (Zhang et al., 2002a). Their
pattern of expression suggests that,
whereas FGFR1 may mediate the
mitogenic effects of FGF2 in the re-
generating cord, FGFR4, whose lev-
els peak at later stages of regener-
ation, may play a role in neural
differentiation. This effect might be
mediated by other FGFs, supporting
multiple and different roles for FGF
signalling during cord regeneration.
These results highlight the impor-
tance and conservation throughout
evolution
of
the
FGF
signalling
mechanism in controlling genera-
tion and number of speciﬁc neu-
rones within different contexts.
The molecular mechanisms induc-
ing up-regulation of FGF2 after injury
of the adult amphibian cord have
yet
to
be
investigated.
Several
genes belonging to the Sox gene
family of transcription factors are in-
duced during neural development,
and some members of this family
have been shown to be upstream of
FGF signalling. It is, therefore, inter-
esting and of potential signiﬁcance
for FGF2 induction in the regenerat-
ing cord that expression of Pleurode-
les Sox1 is up-regulated during re-
generation
(Ferretti
et
al.,
2001).
Nonetheless, systematic analysis of
known putative pathways that may
be permissive for spinal cord regen-
eration in urodeles together with
identiﬁcation of additional candi-
dates by comparison of gene pro-
ﬁles
in
normal
and
regenerating
cords will have to be adopted to
thoroughly address and signiﬁcantly
advance our understanding of how
spinal cord regeneration can be
triggered and accomplished.
Notwithstanding the high conser-
vation of molecular pathways with
evolution, differences in the end re-
sult (being newt or human) occur,
and must ultimately arise from varia-
tions in the molecules involved or dif-
ferences in their deployment. The
same is likely to apply to the mech-
anisms underlying responses to in-
jury. For example, whereas in am-
phibians, FGF2 appears to play a
positive role in expanding a cell
population with neuronal potential,
in mammals FGF2 can be not only a
positive effector of regeneration by
increasing neuronal survival, but also
a negative one, as it is a key medi-
ator of glial responses after spinal
trauma. Application of FGF2 to rat
brain can directly trigger gliotic re-
sponses, potentially contributing to
the inhibitory environment of the
CNS for axonal re-growth (Eclancher
et al., 1996). Furthermore, FGF2 up-
regulation after CNS injury in young
rats occurs at greater levels after the
developmental onset of the scarring
response (Smith et al., 2001). One
view one can take is that activation
of a glial population occurs both in
the
amphibian
(ependymoglial
cells) and mammalian cord (astro-
cytes) after injury. The key difference
could be that in ependymoglial cells
FGF2-induced
proliferation
is
ac-
companied, either directly or indi-
rectly, by a “change” in differentia-
tion potential of these cells, whereas
mammalian astrocytes have lost the
ability to undergo this step. There-
fore, important lessons that may ulti-
mately allow us to stimulate regen-
eration in higher vertebrates can be
learned by studying the mecha-
nisms underlying this striking phe-
nomenon
in
urodele
amphibians
and by thorough comparative anal-
ysis to identify key differences with
mammalian systems, which might
turn out to be quite subtle and pos-
sibly more quantitative than qualita-
tive.
LOSS OF REGENERATIVE
CAPABILITY IN HIGHER
VERTEBRATES WITH
DEVELOPMENT
Chick and Opossum
It has been known for some time
that the immature nervous system is
able to regenerate after injury to a
much greater extent than adult sys-
tems.
Newborn
marsupials
and
avian embryos can allow one to ex-
amine the occurrence of regenera-
tion and its failure within the same
species,
providing
clues
to
the
mechanisms responsible for abortive
regeneration in the adult. To date,
however, these models of spinal
cord regeneration remain underex-
ploited, and little is known of the cel-
lular and molecular events underly-
ing loss of regenerative ability with
development in these systems.
The most studied mammalian re-
generating system is the opossum.
At the time of birth, the extent of
development
of
the
opossum
is
roughly equivalent to that of an em-
bryonic day (E) 14 mouse. Opossums
can regenerate the spinal cord at
birth; this ability is maintained for a
few days after birth (postnatal day
[P] 7–8), but then decreases with the
progression of development. In Mo-
nodelphi domestica, it is probably
largely lost around P12–P14, but spe-
250 FERRETTI ET AL.

ciﬁc assessment of the transition be-
tween regeneration-permissive and
-nonpermissive stages has been im-
paired by maternal cannibalism of
operated P14–P18 pups (Saunders
et al., 1998). It has been shown, how-
ever, that in Didelphis virginiana,
some axons have the ability to re-
grow after injury for as long as 26
days postnatally, suggesting differ-
ences in the maintenance of regen-
erative ability with time among dif-
ferent neuronal populations (Wang
et al., 1998b; Martin et al., 2000; Ter-
man et al., 2000).
The chick is also capable of effec-
tive repair and functional recovery
(Fig. 5), providing the injury occurs
before a pivotal point in embryo-
genesis, E13 (Shimizu et al., 1990).
Functional recovery has been as-
sessed either by the ability of the
chick to hatch unaided or by elec-
trophysiological techniques (Hasan
et al., 1993). By injecting horseradish
peroxidase caudal to sites of injury in
the chick spinal cord during the per-
missive period for regeneration, ret-
rogradely
labelled
neuronal
cell
bodies have been detected in the
brainstem and in more cranial re-
gions of the spinal cord, indicating
that these neurons do extend pro-
cesses beyond the injury site. This re-
generation is observed in chicks at
E5, E8, and E10, but not in chicks at
E15 (Shimizu et al., 1990; Hasan et al.,
1991).
Retrograde double labelling studies
in E10 to E15 embryos have demon-
strated crucially that the regenerative
effects seen are due to regrowth of
existing nerve ﬁbres rather than exten-
sion of late-developing brainstem–spi-
nal connections, consistent with the
fact that, by E10, the brainstem–spinal
projections
are
complete
(Okado
and Oppenheim, 1985; Hasan et al.,
1993). A ﬁrst injection with a ﬂuores-
cent marker was made in a lumbar
location to label all descending ax-
onal ﬁbres, and a second injection
with a different dye was made in the
lumbar spine after a thoracic spinal
injury and 7–8 days of regeneration.
This second injection was caudal to
the injury site, thus only neuronal cell
bodies
whose
axons
had
grown
through the injury site would be retro-
gradely labelled with the second
marker. The detection of double-la-
belled axons has shown that the same
neurons have axonal projections to
the lumbar region of the spinal cord
before and after injury, indicating that
true regeneration has occurred. Re-
cent electrophysiological experiments
have conﬁrmed that functional neu-
ronal circuitry is re-established after
spinal transection during permissive
stages of development (Sholomenko
and Delaney, 1998).
Double-tracing experiments have
been carried out also in the opos-
sum spinal cord, and as in the chick,
it has been shown that, during the
permissive stages, regeneration is in-
deed due to newly grown ﬁbers
rather than to the progression of de-
velopment (Varga et al., 1996; Lepre
et al., 1998; Saunders et al., 1998;
Wang et al., 1998a; Martin et al.,
2000). These authors have shown
that, in addition to anatomical re-
covery, there is a remarkable func-
tional recovery. Interestingly an early
lesion of the opossum cord appears
to precondition the system and im-
prove the response to injury induced
in the adult spinal cord in compari-
son to cords that receive their ﬁrst
injury when fully mature.
Most studies carried out so far on
spinal cord regeneration in higher
vertebrate embryos have almost ex-
clusively focused on axonal regener-
ation.
Whether
replenishment
of
neurones
through
proliferation
of
precursor cells occurs, and plays any
signiﬁcant role in regeneration of
embryonic spinal cord are important
questions that have yet to be thor-
oughly addressed. In the opossum, it
has been shown that there is a pro-
liferative response after injury, al-
though there is no indication of
which cells are dividing and whether
cell proliferation is a requisite for re-
generation to occur (Terman et al.,
2000).
In
preliminary
experiments
where proliferating cell nuclear anti-
gen (PCNA) labelling was used to
assess proliferation, we have not ob-
served striking differences between
normal
and
injured
chick
spinal
cords. PCNA expression, however, is
still widespread at E12–E15, and its
expression is detectable over a sig-
niﬁcant portion of the cell cycle
(throughout S and M phase and in
cells that have divided recently).
Therefore, induction of proliferation
in speciﬁc cell populations might
have been overlooked. The use of
the mitosis marker H3 (phosphory-
lated form of histone 3), which labels
only a few cells at any of the time
points we have examined between
E8 and E17, or BrdU injection in con-
junction with cell-type speciﬁc mark-
ers should allow the issue of recruit-
ment of new cells with neuronal
potential in the regenerate to be
addressed.
Different Responses to Injury in
Regenerating and
Nonregenerating Chick Spinal
Cord
When the chick spinal cord loses the
ability to regenerate, characteristic
features
thoroughly
described
in
both human and rat, such as degen-
eration of severed axons, the devel-
opment of ﬂuid-ﬁlled cavities within
the injured cord and formation of a
glia scar, that consists mainly of as-
trocytes and some connective tissue
elements, are observed (Clearwa-
ters, 1954; Ribchester et al., 1995;
Taoka and Okajima, 1998; Fitch and
Silver, 1999; Kakulas, 1999). Cavita-
tion is thought largely to occur as a
consequence of progressive hyp-
oxia and necrosis within the dam-
aged cord (Kao, 1980), but inﬂam-
matory responses due to persistent
macrophage
activity
have
also
been
implicated
in
the
process
(Matsuyama et al., 1998; Fitch and
Silver, 1999). Although the presence
of a glial scar is considered to be a
key
factor
in
blocking
axonal
growth, either by acting as a physi-
cal barrier or by secreting inhibitory
factors, it should be noted that there
are species, such as the lamprey,
that do regenerate their spinal cord
notwithstanding formation of a glial
scar, which in these animals, seems
to have a positive role in supporting
axonal regrowth (Lurie and Selzer,
1991).
Haemorrhage is another feature
commonly seen after spinal cord in-
jury and may contribute to second-
ary
damage
processes.
Spinal
trauma will frequently cause com-
promise of the blood–spinal cord
barrier, resulting in exposure of blood
components to the nervous system.
Blood contains many neurotoxic el-
OCCURRENCE OF SPINAL CORD REGENERATION 251

ements, and many blood proteins,
including those involved in clotting
cascades, are known to be neuro-
toxic. Breakdown of blood supply to
the spinal cord can also result in hyp-
oxia, which is likely to result in ne-
crotic death of cells, and may be
particularly relevant to cavity forma-
tion (Velardo et al., 2000).
We have recently found that both
the extent of cell death by apoptosis
and of haemorrhage (Fig. 5) are
much
higher
at
developmental
stages nonpermissive for regenera-
tion (e.g., E15) than at permissive
ones (e.g., E11; O’Neill and Ferretti,
2000) O’Neill and Ferretti, manu-
script
in
preparation).
At
earlier
stages of development apoptotic
cells are observed only close to the
injured site. In contrast, at stages
nonpermissive for regeneration, the
apoptotic response spreads a long
distance from the site of primary in-
jury and cavitation occurs in these
animals (Fig. 6). It is conceivable that
there is a causal relationship be-
tween the extent of haemorrhage
and apoptosis, because when the
amount of blood present at the in-
jured site is increased by pharmaco-
logic treatment, the extent of apo-
ptosis
increases.
Developmental
changes that may be relevant to
these responses are changes in the
levels of putative survival factors
(e.g., FGF2 expression in the spinal
cord is developmentally regulated),
the extent of vascularization of the
developing
spinal
cord,
and
changes in “death” proteins, or their
receptors, either in the blood or
within the spinal cord itself.
Serine proteases, such as thrombin
and plasminogen activators, are in-
deed good candidates for contrib-
uting to the increase in cell death
observed in E15 embryos (Gingrich
and Traynelis, 2000). These mole-
cules are present in blood and are
also
produced
within
the
CNS,
where they appear to be implicated
in its development and plasticity
(Turgeon and Houenou, 1997). Sev-
eral serine proteases seem to be up-
regulated after traumatic injury of
the rat cord, and some of them can
induce apoptosis in cultured chick
spinal motoneurons by activating a
caspase-dependent pathway (Smir-
nova et al., 1998; Turgeon et al.,
1998; Citron et al., 2000). Thrombin,
for example, can promote apoptosis
of neuronal cells, although at low
concentrations can be protective
(Nishino et al., 1993; Donovan et al.,
1997; Turgeon et al., 1998). Consis-
tent with the apoptotic effects of
thrombin, activation of its receptor
PAR-1 results in motoneuron death in
E7 chick spinal cord (Turgeon and
Houenou, 1997; Turgeon et al., 1999).
In addition, a thrombin activity that
can induce re-entry in the cell cycle
of mature differentiated tissues has
been reported in regenerating tis-
sues of the newt, such as lens and
limb (Brockes and Kumar, 2002), sug-
gesting that such activity could be
crucial for the initiation of regenera-
tion in several tissues. It will be impor-
tant to assess whether this is the case
also for neural tissue in the newt
(e.g., spinal cord and retina) and
whether such a mechanism might
play a role also in regeneration at
early
stages
of
development
in
higher vertebrates.
Other serine proteases that may
mediate neural cell death and sec-
ondary tissue damage are plasmino-
gen activators (Anthony and Perry,
1998; Flavin et al., 2000; Kataoka et al.,
2000). We have observed that uroki-
nase plasminogen activator mRNA
signiﬁcantly increases in the chick spi-
nal cord after E13, when the spinal
cord looses its ability to regenerate
(O’Neill and Ferretti, unpublished ob-
servations). Within the mouse CNS, tis-
sue plasminogen activator is primarily
produced by microglial cells but is
also found in neurons (Carroll et al.,
1994; Tsirka et al., 1997). Plasminogen
and plasminogen activator–deﬁcient
mice are resistant to kainate-induced
neurodegeneration
(Tsirka
et
al.,
1997). Interestingly, also expression of
certain serine protease inhibitors is de-
velopmentally regulated in the CNS,
and one of these molecules, nexin-1
(PN-1), is up-regulated after periph-
eral nerve lesion in rats (Meier et al.,
1989; Mansuy et al., 1993; Houenou et
al., 1995; Osterwalder et al., 1996). It
will be important to assess the poten-
tial contribution of serine proteases
and their inhibitors in the transition be-
tween regeneration-permissive and
-nonpermissive
stages
of
develop-
ment. Given the signiﬁcant knowl-
edge and pharmacologic tools al-
ready available in the ﬁeld, if these
molecules turn out to be key players in
this transition, they can provide good
targets of intervention for effective
early treatment of spinal injuries.
Myelin-Associated Proteins as
Inhibitors of Spinal Cord
Regeneration
In the chick, the transitional period
for loss of regeneration coincides
with the onset of myelination of the
spinal
cord
(Macklin
and
Weill,
1985). In the opossum, where the re-
generative ability seems to become
dramatically reduced at approxi-
mately P14, the onset of myelin ba-
sic protein expression has been re-
ported
to
occur
around
P15
(Ghooray and Martin, 1993). This
ﬁnding is consistent with much work
suggesting a key role of myelin pro-
teins as inhibitors of regeneration
(Fitch and Silver, 1999; Qiu et al.,
2000; Fouad et al., 2001; Skaper et
al., 2001). Several myelin proteins,
such as myelin-associated glycopro-
tein (MAG), GalC (galactocerebro-
side), and proteins recognized by
the monoclonal antibody IN-1, that
have been recently isolated and
characterized
by
several
groups
and called Nogo (Chen et al., 2000;
GrandPre et al., 2000; Prinjha et al.,
2000), have been shown to have in-
hibitory
activity
on
regeneration.
Support to the possibility that transi-
tion from permissive to restrictive
stages of regeneration is causally re-
lated to myelination has come from
experiments in which myelin protein
activity was blocked by speciﬁc an-
tibodies.
In chick embryos injected with
anti-GalC antibodies and comple-
ment proteins at E9–E12 to delay the
process of myelination, the transition
from permissive to restrictive states is
delayed and does not occur until
E15 (Keirstead et al., 1992). Similar
results have been obtained in post-
hatching chicks after spinal injury
and subsequent disruption of myelin
structure within the spinal cord. In-
jection of antibodies raised against
GalC, or O4, along with injection of
serum complement proteins results
in unravelling of myelin sheaths, a
process that is reversible after cessa-
tion of treatment (Keirstead et al.,
252 FERRETTI ET AL.

1995). After 2–4 weeks of recovery, 9
to 14% of descending brainstem–spi-
nal connections have regenerated
across the lesion site, as detected by
retrograde
double-labelling
tech-
niques. No recovery of voluntary lo-
comotion, however, has been ob-
served
in
any
treated
chick,
although electrophysiological anal-
ysis has indicated that some con-
nections have been re-established
(Keirstead et al., 1995). In the opos-
sum, an inhibitory role of myelination
has also been proposed. After treat-
ment of isolated spinal cord prepa-
ration from P13–P17 opossums with
the IN-1 antibody, ﬁber outgrowth
comparable to that achieved in
younger animals is observed (Varga
et al., 1995). A similar strategy has
achieved comparable results in ex-
periments performed on the adult
rat, promoting the idea that general
myelin disruption may promote en-
hanced axonal regeneration after
spinal injury in all vertebrate systems
(Dyer et al., 1998; Fitch and Silver,
1999; Qiu et al., 2000; Fouad et al.,
2001; Skaper et al., 2001).
There is also evidence, however,
to suggest that lack of myelin pro-
teins alone is not sufﬁcient for the
occurrence of regeneration as mice
lacking MAGs do not seem to regen-
erate signiﬁcantly better than their
wild-type counterparts and the abil-
ity of retinal ganglion cells to extend
neurites on optic nerves is lost before
the optic nerve becomes myelin-
ated (Shewan et al., 1995; Bartsch,
1996; Fitch and Silver, 1999; Qiu et
al., 2000). This is also the case in the
sheep embryo, in which spinal cord
regeneration does not appear to
occur even if it is injured at early
developmental
stages
(roughly
equivalent to P1 in the opossum) be-
fore the onset of myelination (Meuli-
Simmen et al., 1996). It is conceiv-
able that, given the larger size of
these embryos, the sheep spinal
cord is more vascularized than that
of chick and rat embryos at compa-
rable
developmental
stages.
It
would be interesting to establish
whether
this
is
the
case,
and
whether after injury of embryonic
sheep cord signiﬁcant haemorrhage
and apoptosis, more comparable to
that observed in E11 than E15 chicks,
occurs.
We recently have investigated the
possibility that developmental regu-
lation of the inhibitory protein Nogo
may contribute to the transition to a
regeneration
incompetent
state.
However, we have found that, in de-
veloping chick and human spinal
cords, both Nogo and its receptor
NgR (Fournier et al., 2001) are ex-
pressed very early during develop-
ment, are present at times when the
spinal cord can regenerate, and are
not down-regulated after injury at
stages permissive for regeneration
(O’Neill and Ferretti, 2002). This ﬁnd-
ing, together with complementary
observations by other colleagues
(Josephson et al., 2001; Huber et al.,
2002), suggests that the presence of
Nogo is not inhibitory per se. Al-
though we cannot exclude that the
inhibitory activity of Nogo is medi-
ated by a yet unidentiﬁed receptor
not expressed early in development,
expression of Nogo in the spinal
cord, where we have found it local-
ized to neurones before it switches
almost completely to oligodendro-
cytes at later stages, would indicate
that changes in its cellular localiza-
tion may be among the factors con-
tributing to loss of regenerative ca-
pability.
The physiological reason(s) behind
the production of inhibitory proteins
around sites of injury is not clear. That
both Nogo and NgR are expressed
very early during development and
that, in the chick, they display a
complementary pattern of expres-
sion, suggests an important role for
the Nogo signaling system in devel-
opment, possibly in axon elongation
or pathﬁnding. All together, it is most
likely that myelin-associated inhibi-
tory molecules play primarily devel-
opmental roles involving stabilization
of the neural network, rather than
having evolved as speciﬁc inhibitors.
After injury they might be used to
seal off damaged regions from nor-
mal tissues and such function might
not be compatible with regenera-
tion.
CONCLUSIONS
The issue of spinal cord regeneration
is a very complex one, and multiple
factors are clearly required for a suc-
cessful outcome. It is evident that
the models of spinal cord regenera-
tion discussed here, adult urodeles
and developing chick and opos-
sum, can provide important clues to-
ward understanding why regenera-
tive
capability
is
lost
through
evolution and development. Such
models can allow one to explore the
possibility
of
restoring
it
at
later
stages of development and in adult
vertebrates by using multiple ap-
proaches. A much more in-depth
knowledge of the molecular mech-
anisms underlying the regenerative
ability of the CNS in these models,
however, will have to be gathered if
we are to exploit these systems to
develop
integrated
strategies
for
therapeutic intervention in humans.
It is a pity, and probably in the long
run short-sighted, that most of the
current work on CNS regeneration is
on
systems
that
“cannot
do
it,”
rather than taking advantage also
of systems that “can do it,” and
might teach us how to make it hap-
pen.

We thank M. Bhutta for helping with
the experiment shown in Figure 5.

256 FERRETTI ET AL.

===== Floriddia2020_NatComm_Oligodendrocyte+SCI =====
ARTICLE
Distinct oligodendrocyte populations have spatial
preference and different responses to spinal
cord injury
Elisa M. Floriddia
1✉, Tânia Lourenço1,2,3, Shupei Zhang1, David van Bruggen
1, Markus M. Hilscher
4,5,
Petra Kukanja
1, João P. Gonçalves dos Santos1, Müge Altınkök1, Chika Yokota4, Enric Llorens-Bobadilla
6,
Sara B. Mulinyawe7, Mário Grãos
2,3, Lu O. Sun
7,8, Jonas Frisén6, Mats Nilsson
4 &
Gonçalo Castelo-Branco
1,9✉
Mature oligodendrocytes (MOLs) show transcriptional heterogeneity, the functional con-
sequences of which are unclear. MOL heterogeneity might correlate with the local envir-
onment or their interactions with different neuron types. Here, we show that distinct MOL
populations have spatial preference in the mammalian central nervous system (CNS). We
found that MOL type 2 (MOL2) is enriched in the spinal cord when compared to the brain,
while MOL types 5 and 6 (MOL5/6) increase their contribution to the OL lineage with age in
all analyzed regions. MOL2 and MOL5/6 also have distinct spatial preference in the spinal
cord regions where motor and sensory tracts run. OL progenitor cells (OPCs) are not spe-
ciﬁed into distinct MOL populations during development, excluding a major contribution of
OPC intrinsic mechanisms determining MOL heterogeneity. In disease, MOL2 and MOL5/6
present different susceptibility during the chronic phase following traumatic spinal cord
injury. Our results demonstrate that the distinct MOL populations have different spatial
preference and different responses to disease.
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-19453-x
OPEN

2 Biocant, Technology Transfer Association, Cantanhede, Portugal. 3 Centre for Neuroscience and Cell Biology (CNC), University of Coimbra,
Coimbra, Portugal. 4 Science for Life Laboratory, Department of Biophysics and Biochemistry, Stockholm University, 17165 Solna, Sweden. 5 Cartana AB,
Nobels väg 16, 17165 Solna, Sweden. 6 Department of Cell and Molecular Biology, Karolinska Institutet, Biomedicum, 17177 Stockholm, Sweden. 7 Department
of Neurobiology, Stanford University School of Medicine, Stanford, CA 94305, USA. 8 Department of Molecular Biology, University of Texas Southwestern
Medical Center, Dallas, TX 75390, USA. 9 Ming Wai Lau Centre for Reparative Medicine, Stockholm Node, Karolinska Institutet, 171 77 Stockholm, Sweden.
✉email: elisa.ﬂoriddia@ki.se; goncalo.castelo-branco@ki.se
NATURE COMMUNICATIONS |  (2020) 11:5860 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-19453-x | www.nature.com/naturecommunications
1
1234567890():,;

M
yelin is produced by oligodendrocytes (OLs); it is highly
enriched in lipids (80% of its dry mass), insulates
(allowing
electrical
conduction)
and
metabolically
supports axons1. Myelin synchronizes the impulse trafﬁc between
distant regions. This is a critical role to guarantee optimal motor,
sensory, and higher-order cognitive functions2. For instance,
cortical electrical impulses can be synchronized. Indeed, the
conduction time between the left and right brain hemispheres is
30 ms, while it is 150–300 ms through unmyelinated short pro-
jecting ﬁbers in the same hemisphere, so that impulses from
different neurons may reach their target neurons in a coordinated
manner when needed3. In addition, myelin proteins directly
control synapse formation by inhibiting axonal sprouting3.
Myelination is important during development and remains
plastic throughout life. In fact, myelin is continuously produced
and modulated by neuronal activity, allowing learning of new
skills4.
We have recently reported that the OL lineage is hetero-
geneous, as it is composed of transcriptionally distinct sub-
populations/states during development and disease5–7. The
heterogeneity of the OL lineage does not lie exclusively within the
OL transcriptome. Indeed, developmentally distinct OPC pools
generate OL lineage cells with different abilities to respond to
demyelination8. Mature OLs (MOLs) form myelin internodes of
various length and thickness, even along the same axon9,10, and
show properties regulated at both cell-autonomous and non-
autonomous levels4,11.
In this study, we asked whether the spatial distribution of
MOLs might correlate with distinct OL functional states or sub-
types. Single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNAseq) unveiled the
transcriptional heterogeneity of the OL lineage and suggested
differential enrichment of MOLs in different regions of the cen-
tral nervous system (CNS)5. The prediction of region enrichment
based on scRNAseq can be biased by technical limitations, such as
differences in viability of cell subpopulations during tissue
dissociation12.
In this study, we used RNAscope in situ hybridization (ISH)
and in situ sequencing (ISS)13 to determine the distribution of OL
lineage populations. We found that distinct MOL populations
present spatial preference in the CNS. The origin of distinct MOL
populations is independent of intrinsic developmental mechan-
isms, but plausibly driven by extrinsic signals, such as neuronal
electrical activity or other cues in the environment where MOLs
reside. Furthermore, distinct MOLs present different responses to
traumatic spinal cord injury (SCI) in the chronic phase, but not in
the acute phase following injury or during demyelination in
experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), a mouse
model of multiple sclerosis. Our study paves the way for a more
sophisticated understanding of the MOL populations-speciﬁc
functional roles in development, health, and disease, possibly
allowing better targeting of the OL subtypes to achieve regen-
eration and repair of the central nervous system.
Results
MOL2 and MOL5/6 show different spatial preference in the
mouse brain and spinal cord. We assessed the distribution of the
OL lineage within white matter (WM) and gray matter (GM) of
the brain and dorsal spinal cord in situ (Supplementary Fig. 1a,
b). We performed immunohistochemistry (IHC) and RNAscope
ISH for Sox10 as a pan marker of the OL lineage and analyzed
confocal images of the corpus callosum (WM), somatosensory
cortex (GM) and the dorsal spinal cord (GM and WM) (Sup-
plementary Fig. 1a, b). We analyzed the images with a custom
automated pipeline (CellProﬁler; Supplementary Fig. 1c–e).
Ptprz1 (receptor-type tyrosine-protein phosphatase zeta 1) is a
marker of OPCs and committed OPCs (COPs)5,6. Sox10+-
Ptprz1+ cells presented a homogeneous distribution across the
analyzed regions (Fig. 1a–c, m, Supplementary Fig. 1f). As
expected, we observed a decreased contribution of OPCs/COPs to
the OL lineage from juvenile to adulthood, especially in the
somatosensory cortex (Fig. 1c, m, Supplementary Fig. 1f). Among
the six transcriptionally distinct mature oligodendrocyte popu-
lations previously described, MOL1, MOL2, and MOL5/6 present
the most distinct gene marker modules5–7; therefore, we analyzed
their spatial distribution in the mouse central nervous system.
Egr2 (Early Growth Response 2; also known as Krox20) is
expressed speciﬁcally by MOL1 in the OL lineage5. We observed a
statistically non-signiﬁcant difference of MOL1 distribution
within the analyzed regions and across ages (Fig. 1d–f, m and
Supplementary Fig. 1g). Klk6 (Kallikrein Related Peptidase 6) is a
distinct marker speciﬁc for MOL2 (Supplementary Fig. 1j)5–7,14.
Klk6 has been previously associated with demyelinating pathol-
ogy in EAE and SCI15,16. Strikingly, we observed that Klk6+
MOL2 is a population speciﬁcally enriched in the dorsal spinal
cord and almost absent in cortex and corpus callosum (Fig. 1g–i,
m and Supplementary Fig. 1h). In contrast, MOL5 and 6 popu-
lations (MOL5/6) that exhibit high expression levels of Ptgds
(Prostaglandin D2 Synthase) (Supplementary Fig. 1j)5–7 showed a
dynamic contribution to the OL lineage, increasing along time
and following the myelination temporal pattern. Indeed, at
juvenile (P20), MOL5/6 are more abundant in the dorsal spinal
cord, where myelination is visible early on after birth (P5-6),
compared to cortex and corpus callosum, where myelination is
visible around P10-15. In adulthood (P60), MOL5/6 is the main
population contributing to the OL lineage in both brain and
spinal cord, being most abundant in the corpus callosum
(Fig. 1j–m and Supplementary Fig. 1i). We conﬁrmed the
described spatial preference based on one population-speciﬁc
marker detected with RNAscope ISH using ISS. ISS is a sequen-
cing technology that allows to enquire the simultaneous expres-
sion of multiple RNAs in tissue sections13. We took advantage of
the higher multiplexing power of ISS compared to RNAscope ISH
to detect OPC/COP, MOL1, MOL2, and MOL5/6 based on the
combination of multiple marker genes (Supplementary Fig. 2, 3
and Supplementary Data 1). MOL2 are enriched in Anxa5, Hopx,
and Klk6 and MOL5/6 have increased expression of Ptdgs, and
also express Grm3 and Car2 (Supplementary Fig. 1j)5. ISS indi-
cates the number of Sox10+/Plp1+ cells also positive for Anxa5/
Klk6 or Anxa5/Hopx (MOL2) in the brain is lower than in the
spinal cord, unlike Sox10+/Plp1+ cells positive for Ptgds/Car2 and
Ptgds/Grm3 (MOL5/6) (Supplementary Fig. 3a and Supplemen-
tary Data 1 and 2). Furthermore, we observed that MOL5/6 are
also increased with age in the cortex and corpus callosum, and
spinal cord (Supplementary Fig. 3a, b and Supplementary Data 1
and 2). Altogether, the ISS data conﬁrmed the spatial preference
and distribution of MOL2 and MOL5/6 we observed by RNA-
scope ISH. In summary, here we described the spatial preference
of the most abundant OL lineage populations and show these
populations can be correctly identiﬁed by individual as well as
multiple speciﬁc marker genes we carefully selected based on our
previous scRNAseq characterization of the lineage5–7,17.
MOL2 preferentially reside in the WM and sensory tracts of the
spinal cord, unlike MOL5/6. Given the enrichment of MOL2 in
the dorsal spinal cord compared to the analyzed brain regions
(Fig. 1i, m), we further investigated whether MOL2 and MOL5/
6 spatial preference relates to distinct regions and functional
tracts in the spinal cord (Fig. 2a). Strikingly, we observed an

3

enrichment of MOL2 in the WM compared to the GM of the
spinal cord (Fig. 2c, d and Supplementary Data 3). On the con-
trary, the MOL5/6 population is enriched in the GM compared to
the WM of the spinal cord (Fig. 2g, h and Supplementary Data 3).
Additionally, we observed that the MOL2 population decreases in
the GM of the spinal cord from juvenile to adulthood (Fig. 2d
and Supplementary Data 3). Also in this instance, we conﬁrmed
the MOL spatial preference by ISS analysis. Indeed, Ptgds+/
Sox10+/Plp1+ or Car2+/Sox10+/Plp1+ cells (MOL5/6) are pre-
sent
in
higher
proportions
in
GM
compared
to
WM,
while Anxa5+/Sox10+/Plp1+, Hopx+/Sox10+/Plp1+. or Klk6+/
Sox10+/Plp1+ cells (MOL2) are more abundant in the WM
compared to the GM of the spinal cord at P20 (Supplementary
Fig. 3c and Supplementary Data 1). Furthermore, analysis of a
recent spatial transcriptomics18 study19 (https://als-st.nygenome.
org/) investigating gene expression changes following amyo-
trophic lateral sclerosis indicates a similar spatial distribution of
Klk6 and Hopx (MOL2) as well as Ptgds (MOL5/6) and Il33 genes
(MOL6) (Supplementary Fig. 3e) as we report here. Despite the
lack of focus on the heterogeneity of the OL lineage, the spatial
distribution of these genes, characteristic of MOL populations,
shown by Maniatis et al.19 and our spatial analysis focused on the
OL lineage populations presented here give conﬁdence that
MOL2 and MOL5/6 populations have distinct distributions
across the CNS in vivo.
In accordance with previous reports20–24, in the dorsal white
matter (dorsal funiculi), the OL lineage density around the
ascending sensory tracts (formed of the fasciculi gracilis and
cuneatus) and dorsal corticospinal tract (motor tracts) correlates
with the development of myelination. Indeed, we observed an
increase in the OL lineage in the dorsal corticospinal tract with
age (Fig. 2b and Supplementary Data 3) and constant distribution
in the sensory tracts (Fig. 2b and Supplementary Data 3). More
importantly, MOL2 are preferentially found where the sensory
tracts run (Fig. 2e, f), while MOL5/6 preferentially localize in the
dorsal corticospinal tract (Fig. 2i, j and Supplementary Data 3). In
the dorsal funiculi, similarly to our observations in the GM and
WM of the spinal cord, MOL2 and MOL5/6 show adjacent
enriched distribution and spatial preference.
Ventrally- and dorsally derived OPCs can give rise to similar
MOLs. The OL lineage derives from distinct progenitor domains
and developmental stages25, which might have a role in the
speciﬁcation of OPCs into distinct MOL populations and there-
fore a role in the observed spatial preference. To investigate this
possibility, we ﬁrst isolated the OL lineage from Emx1::Cre-
Sox10::GFP-TdTom mice26 and performed scRNAseq on ven-
trally (eGFP+) and dorsally (TdTom+) derived OL lineage cells.
We analyzed the P60 corpus callosum (Fig. 3a) since this region
has representation of OLs derived from both the cortical plate
(dorsal domain) and the lateral and medial ganglionic eminences
(ventral domains of the embryonic forebrain)26. As expected26, a
greater proportion of TdTom+ OL lineage cells was obtained
(Fig. 3a, c and Supplementary Data 4). Graph-based clustering
(Seurat)27,28 of 2853 OL lineage cells and label transfer from the
Marques et al. scRNA-Seq dataset5 led to the identiﬁcation of the
previously identiﬁed OL lineage subpopulations (Fig. 3b and
Supplementary Fig. 4a, b)5,17. Importantly, the contribution of
ventrally- and dorsally derived OL lineage cells to each cluster
was comparable (Fig. 3c, d), suggesting that the developmental
waves have similar potential to generate the transcriptionally
distinct OL lineage subpopulations.
In the brain, the ventrally derived and dorsally derived OL
lineage cells arise during embryonic and early-postnatal devel-
opment, respectively25,29. We thus further performed lineage
tracing using the Pdgfrα::CreERTM-loxP-RCE (Z/EG) mouse
model30 to fate map the progeny of pre-OPCs appearing at E13.56
or OPCs at P3-5 (Fig. 3e). Postnatal recombination of the Pdgfrα::
CreERTM-GFP mice results in the assessment of OPCs arising at
both
postnatal
stages
and
remaining
OPCs,
while
E12.5
recombination allows the assessment of ventral pre-OPCs arising
from the ﬁrst developmental wave. In the spinal cord, ventrally
derived and dorsally derived OL lineage cells arise from two
distinct embryonic stages of development (E13.5 and E15.5)31;
therefore, our recombination strategy at E12.5 will label the pre-
OPCs appearing at E13.5 in the spinal cord, while recombination
at P3-5 will label OPCs derived from both embryonic timepoints
that have not undergone differentiation yet and OPCs of
postnatal origin (P0) (Fig. 3e), respectively.

from Emx1::Cre-SOX10::GFP-TdTom mouse. Dashed outline highlights the corpus callosum and dissected region used for scRNAseq. b, c Uniform Manifold
Approximation and Projection (UMAP) plots showing the OL lineage composition determined by graph-based clustering (Seurat)27,28 and integration cells
with the Marques et al. scRNA-Seq dataset5 (b) and the TdTom+ and GFP+ OL lineage cells contribution to the clusters (c). d Frequency distribution of
the TdTom+ and GFP+ OL lineage cells forming the major clusters. n = 2853 cells. e Schematic overview of the fate mapping experimental design. Green
arrows show the GFP expression timeline 24 h delayed from the time of tamoxifen injection. f, g Percentage of the fate mapped OL lineage cells (Sox10+-
GFP+ cells) derived by OPCs labeled at E12.5 and P3-5 calculated on the total number of OL lineage cells (Sox10+ cells) at juvenile (f) and adulthood
(g). Data are presented as mean ± SEM. n = 4−5 (f) and 3−5 (g) animals per condition, and can be assessed in the Source Data ﬁle. h, i Confocal

(h) and P60 (i) corpus callosum. Scale bar = 20 μm. j Percentages of the fate mapped OPCs-COPs (Ptprz1+-GFP+ OL lineage cells), MOL1 (Egr2+-GFP+

fate mapped OL lineage cells (Sox10+-GFP+ cells) in the juvenile and adult corpus callosum. Data are presented as mean ± SEM. n = 3−5 animals per
condition, and can be assessed in the Source Data ﬁle. Asterisks indicate a signiﬁcant difference between conditions (**p ≤0.01, ****p ≤0.0001, two-way

NATURE COMMUNICATIONS |  (2020) 11:5860 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-19453-x | www.nature.com/naturecommunications
5

Our recombination strategy labeled the majority of the lineage
in the juvenile CNS (95.43 ± 6.22% of the Sox10+ cells were
positive for the eGFP reporter in the cortex, 62.24 ± 2.35% in
corpus callosum, and 96.05 ± 1.15% spinal cord, Fig. 3f), similarly
to previous reports5,6,26,32. We also observed sparser labeling in
adulthood (62.38 ± 19.9% of the Sox10+ cells were positive for the
eGFP reporter in the cortex, 44.16 ± 9.04% in the corpus
callosum, and 24.32 ± 8.26% in the dorsal horns; Fig. 3g). The
considerable contribution and expansion of non-recombined late
embryonic (E15.5 derived) and adult OPCs (GFP−generated
within the process of life-long continuous addition of new
myelinating OLs, for myelin homeostasis and in response to
experience) might explain the relatively low labeling we observed
in the juvenile corpus callosum and in all analyzed regions during
adulthood33,34. Importantly, we observed that a subset of E12.5
pre-OPCs and their progeny do not disappear later in life in any
of the analyzed regions (Fig. 3f, g and Supplementary Fig. 5),
unlike previously reported25,26,29.
Consistent with our scRNA-Seq data, we did not observe
signiﬁcant difference in the relative contribution of the two
developmental waves to the generation of MOL1, MOL2, and
MOL5/6 populations in the brain and spinal cord (Fig. 3h, j and
Supplementary Fig. 5a–m). While most of the MOL2 in the dorsal
horns of the spinal cord were derived from E12.5 pre-OPCs at
P20, the contribution from postnatally fate mapped OPCs
reached comparable levels in adulthood (19.09 ± 4.95% and
23.35 ± 1.28% from the postnatally- and embryonically derived
MOL2, respectively; Supplementary Fig. 5m–o). This suggests
that postnatally labeled OPCs start differentiating into MOL2
later than embryonically derived pre-OPCs.
TFEB does not regulate the generation of MOL2 population.
Our scRNAseq and lineage tracing data suggest that the domain
and time of developmental origin does not inﬂuence the OPC
speciﬁcation towards distinct MOL populations. Therefore, we
assessed whether other intrinsic mechanisms might drive the
region speciﬁcity of MOL subtypes. Recently, it has been shown
that the transcription factor EB (TFEB), highly expressed in
premyelinating oligodendrocytes35, cell-autonomously regulates
programmed cell death in the OL lineage and as such TFEB
spatiotemporally controls the CNS myelination during develop-
ment36. We conditionally knock-out TFEB taking advantage of
the Olig2::Cre-TFEB mouse model36. We collected juvenile spinal
cords from control and conditional KO (cKO) littermates and
performed RNAscope ISH to detect the OL lineage (Sox10+ cells),
mature OLs (Aspa+ cells)5, MOL2 (Klk6+-Aspa+ cells), and
MOL5/6 (Ptgds+- Aspa+ cells) in the gray and white matter of the
spinal cord. We observed a consistent contribution of the mature
OLs (Aspa+-Sox10+ cells) to the lineage (Sox10+ cells) in both
genotypes (Supplementary Fig. 6a–c) in the spinal cord gray
(Supplementary Fig. 6b) and white matter (Supplementary
Fig. 6c). We also observed that all the Aspa+ cells express Sox10,
conﬁrming that Aspa is a marker gene speciﬁc for the OL
lineage5.
When we analyzed the effect of the deletion of TFEB on the
MOL2 and MOL5/6 populations, the number of Aspa+ cells,
MOL2 and MOL5/6 suggest a different representation of MOLs
in the mutant mice (Fig. 4e). This was particularly evident in the
gray matter of the Olig2::Cre+-TFEBﬂ/ﬂspinal cord tissue.
Indeed, here we observed a low percentage of MOL5/6 and
their more prominent reduction compared to Aspa+ cells (Fig. 4a,
b, d, e). To note, the GM of the spinal cord is a region where
MOL5/6 are very abundant in WT (Fig. 2g, h) as well as in Olig2::
Cre−-TFEBﬂ/ﬂcontrol littermate mice (Fig. 3a, b, d). The
percentage of the MOL5/6 calculated over the Aspa+ cells in
the WM of Olig2::Cre+-TFEBﬂ/ﬂand Olig2::Cre−-TFEBﬂ/ﬂspinal
cord did not show statistically signiﬁcant changes (Fig. 4d, e).
Similarly, the percentage of the MOL2 over the Aspa+ cells in
GM and WM of the spinal cord was comparable between
genotypes (Fig. 4a–c, e). Our results suggest that TFEB-dependent
programmed cell death of premyelinating OLs does not have a
regulatory role in the speciﬁcation of MOL populations in the
spinal cord.
To further assess whether brain- and spinal cord-derived OPCs
are intrinsically programmed to differentiate into MOL2 or
MOL5/6, we also used an in vitro assay, the microﬁber assay, as
previously described11,37. The microﬁbers mimic an inert axonal
net (3D environment); therefore, this assay has been used to
evaluate the intrinsic potential of OPCs to generate OLs with
different morphological features11,37. Here, we used it to
determine
the OPC
intrinsic
differentiation
potential.
We
collected brains and spinal cords from P4-6 pups, dissociated
the tissue and MACS-sorted for OPCs to seed on microﬁbers with
a diameter range of 2–4 μm. We differentiated brain and spinal
cord-derived OPCs separately for 3, 7, and 14 days in vitro and
visualized the OL lineage and MOL-speciﬁc populations by ICC
and RNAscope ISH to detect MBP and PDGFRα, as well as Itrp2,
Dusp1, Klk6 or Ptgds, respectively. MBP+ cells constitute
approximately 10–20% of the cells cultured on the microﬁbers
(Fig. 4f–h) at 3, 7, and 14 days of in vitro differentiation with no
differences in the percentage of MBP+ cells between timepoints
in brain (p value 0.185) and spinal cord cultures (p value 0.8074)
(Fig. 4f–h), Additionally, the morphological complexity of MBP+
cells increases along time, suggesting that MBP+ cells are
differentiating into mature OLs (Fig. 4f, g, j, k). The time-
course in brain-derived cultures showed that PDGFRα+ OPCs
were approximately 15% of the cells at 3 days and decrease
considerably (p value 0.0363) at 14 days (Supplementary Fig. 6d,
g). We also observed that 40% of the cells in brain-derived
cultures at 3 days were newly formed OLs (NFOLs, Itrp2+), with
a signiﬁcant increase to nearly 60% (p value 0.019) at 14 days
(Supplementary Fig. 6f, g). 40–60% of the MBP+ cells were Itrp2+
(Supplementary Fig. 6f, h), indicating that a large proportion of
OLs had not transitioned to an MOL state.
When we quantiﬁed the percentage of Dusp1+- MBP+
(MOL1), Klk6+- MBP+ (MOL2) and Ptgds+- MBP+ (MOL5/6)
cells wrapping the microﬁbers, we did not observe any Klk6+-
MBP+ cells in culture (Fig. 4f, g, i, Supplementary Fig. 6d, e, g, h).
While we could detect rare Dusp1+-MBP+ cells at 14 days
(Supplementary Fig. 6e, h), the vast majority of Dusp1+ were not
MOLs, but most likely OPCs and VLMCs, consistent with the
expression of Dusp1 also in these populations5. Instead, we
observed around 40% Ptgds+- MBP+ cells in both brain and
spinal cord OL lineage cultures (Fig. 4f–i). These data suggest that
OPCs mainly differentiate into NFOLs and MOL5/6, but not into
MOL1 or MOL2 in standard culture conditions11,37. Altogether
our in vivo and in vitro data suggest that OPCs are not
intrinsically programmed to generate distinct MOL subpopula-
tions. Therefore, more plausibly, the exposure of OPCs to
extrinsic
signals
during
critical
windows
of
migration
or
differentiation during development might regulate the differen-
tiation potential of OPCs.
MOL2 and MOL5/6 have different responses to traumatic
spinal cord injury. The spatial preference of MOL2 and MOL5/6
for different regions and tracts of the spinal cord might relate to
functional differences in disease. Traumatic injury of the spinal
cord is a chronic pathological condition that leads to loss of
locomotor and sensory functions largely due to Wallerian
degeneration38. Following injury, myelin is lost at the injury site
ARTICLE
NATURE COMMUNICATIONS | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-19453-x
6
NATURE COMMUNICATIONS |  (2020) 11:5860 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-19453-x | www.nature.com/naturecommunications

and partially around the axons spared by the injury. We took
advantage of the dorsal funiculi transection, a mild model of
traumatic injury (Fig. 5a, b). We observed that the OL lineage is
well represented distal to and at the injury site, both during acute
(14 days post-injury, Fig. 5d) and chronic (3- and 5 months post-
injury, Fig. 5f, h, i) phases following injury39–43. During the acute
phase following injury, when part of the OL lineage undergoes
cell death, we observed that MOL2 and MOL5/6 contribute to the
OL lineage at the injury site in a similar manner (Fig. 5c, e). In
contrast, during the chronic phase (when remyelination occurs),
we observed a decreased contribution of MOL2 to the OL lineage
at the injury site (Fig. 5f, g, j, k). While MOL5/6 reached a higher
contribution to the OL lineage (Fig. 5f, g, j, k) compared to the
naive spinal cord (Fig. 2), despite the absence of intact axons in
the injury site.
We also assessed the spatial distribution of MOL2 and MOL5/6
in regions of Wallerian degeneration rostral and caudal to the
injury site (Fig. 5f, g, j, k and Supplementary Fig. 7a–e). We
observed that the spatial preference of MOL2 and MOL5/6 for the
dorsal columns and dorsal corticospinal tract was unaffected by

6 in the dorsal funiculi rostral and caudal to the injury did not

remained similar to their contribution in the white matter of the
intact adult spinal cord (Fig. 2 and Supplementary Data 3).
Surprisingly, the contribution of MOL2 to the OL lineage in the
same regions increased with time (Fig. 5j, k). Indeed, 5 mpi, the
MOL2 cell density in WD is even greater than found in the WM
of the intact adult spinal cord (Fig. 2 and Supplementary Data 3).
We also investigated the dynamics of MOLs following severe
contusion, another model of traumatic injury (Fig. 5l, m). As in
