88 |   
FRANKLIN
cell-­cell signaling, and matrix remodeling. Furthermore, fibroblasts 
retained this anatomically-­specific gene expression program, which 
included HOX genes, in the absence of heterotopic signaling.14 This 
“positional memory” suggests a large degree of developmental het-
erogeneity and memory in mesenchymal cells and may provide a co-
ordinate system of positional identity. The contribution of ontogeny 
and positional information to fibroblast function will be revisited in 
a later section.
The structural role of fibroblasts as scaffolds is best observed 
in primary19,20 and secondary16,21 lymphoid tissues. Migration of 
innate and adaptive immune cells to their distinct niches in lymph 
nodes is facilitated by specialized and spatially defined fibroblastic 
cells of mesenchymal origin, the aforementioned FRCs. These cells 
include multiple subsets, each located in distinct zones of the lymph 
node.16 These fibroblastic scaffolds guide immune cells, including 
dendritic cells, T cells, and B cells, to their appropriate locations. For 
example, naïve T cells and dendritic cells presenting cognate antigen 
both migrate along this structure, thus increasing their chances of 
meeting and activating adaptive immune responses.22,23
Taking all of these structural functions of fibroblasts into ac-
count, this fundamental and ubiquitous cell type can also be defined 
as a “supportive” cell within tissues. As recently discussed else-
where, cells within a given tissue fall into two functional categories, 
(a) primary cells, responsible for performing the primary function of 
the tissue, and (b) supportive cells, responsible for performing sup-
portive functions, which facilitate the execution of the primary func-
tion.24 To illustrate this point, fibroblasts in the lung act to support 
proper organ functioning by providing ECM with structural proper-
ties that allow for continuous inspiration and expiration.25
2.2  |  Cellular dynamics
Since fibroblasts help provide positional information to other cell 
types within tissues, it comes as no surprise that they are largely ses-
sile under steady-­state conditions.26 Indeed, migratory cells would 
not be best suited to serve as signposts or scaffolds. Fibroblasts also 
demonstrate low rates of turnover in the absence of inflammatory 
or activating signals.27 However, under certain settings such as in-
flammation, fibroblasts increase both their migratory and prolifera-
tive potential. Fibroblasts and specifically myofibroblasts (discussed 
in more detail below) accumulate in the tissue from a variety of 
sources including proliferation of local mesenchymal cells,28 infiltra-
tion of precursors from the bone marrow,10 and via a process called 
epithelial-­mesenchymal transition (EMT), wherein epithelial cells 
transdifferentiate into fibroblast-­like cells.29 Fibroblast proliferation 
of both recruited and resident cells is observed during the wound-­
healing response.30 In this setting, fibroblasts proliferate in response 
to inflammatory signals such as TGF-­β and undergo profound meta-
bolic changes to allow for population expansion and increased ECM 
deposition.31 Another example of fibroblast proliferation occurs 
in the lymph node, an organ that can expand tenfold during adap-
tive immune responses, yet still maintains structure and integrity. 
Expansion is partially due to FRC network relaxation,32 but is also 
due to proliferation of fibroblasts.32-­35
Following tissue injury, fibroblasts can also engage a unique pro-
cess that does not involve proliferation or recruitment of cells. In the 
skin, it was found that following local fibroblast loss, the nuclei of 
neighboring fibroblasts remain fixed in position.26 Fibroblasts were 
found to extend into the depleted region, without migration of the 
main cell body, to maintain what the authors called “positional stabil-
ity.” It is possible that this ability of fibroblasts to redirect processes 
to “cover more ground” in sites of injury explains why proliferation 
and/or recruitment does not always take place. This mechanism begs 
the question; why would fibroblasts cover space in this way rather 
than through proliferation or recruitment? In addition to this strategy 
being more energy efficient, another possible explanation is that the 
location in which a fibroblast resides has important meaning, adding 
additional data to support the role of fibroblasts as signposts within 
the tissue. Indeed, movement of fibroblast cell bodies may disrupt fi-
broblast patterning and cause lasting effects on tissue function. The 
sections above highlight the core functions and cellular dynamics of 
fibroblasts with some reference to the signals driving these activi-
ties. The following section will expand on the factors that influence 
the phenotype and function of fibroblasts within tissues.
2.3  |  Determinants of cellular function
Fibroblasts were traditionally thought to be a largely homogenous 
population. However, this paradigm was challenged by a number 
of studies showing phenotypic heterogeneity in fibroblast popula-
tions both between different tissues and within the same tissue.36 
Genetic studies have identified a number of distinct fibroblast func-
tions, and both bulk and single cell sequencing data have allowed 
an even greater appreciation for the degree of transcriptional het-
erogeneity that exists for fibroblasts. This feature of transcriptional 
heterogeneity is also shared by macrophages; however, the route to 
this heterogeneity is quite different between these two cell types 
(Figure 1).
As was discussed above, key determinants for fibroblast identity 
are ontogeny and positional information acquired during develop-
ment (Figure 1). These cell-­intrinsic factors provide fibroblasts with 
the identity and transcriptional program to serve their functions as 
signposts and scaffolds in different tissues, especially in communica-
tion with epithelial cells.1 This is in contrast to macrophages, which 
are more affected by cell-­extrinsic factors such as tissue-­specific 
environmental cues.37 However, this distinction is not absolute, but 
rather aims to highlight the dominant determinants of cellular iden-
tity for the two cell types. Certainly, fibroblasts, like macrophages, 
are also responsive to cell-­extrinsic signals such as inflammatory 
and mechanical cues.12,38 The resulting functional polarization in 
response to signals within the environment adds an additional layer 
of complexity to fibroblast functions and intercellular interactions.
A well-­characterized example of fibroblast functional polar-
ization in response to environmental cues is the myofibroblast.39 
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

    |  89
FRANKLIN
While fibroblasts can have contractile functions during steady state 
as exemplified by the FRC network within secondary lymphoid tis-
sues,21 contractility is an inducible function in other tissue fibroblast 
populations. Inducible contractility is especially important in wound 
healing where fibroblasts migrate into the wound bed to assist with 
wound closure and tissue regeneration.28 This activity is thought 
to progress through the transition from relatively weak adhesions 
with the surrounding ECM to strong adhesions necessary for the 
generation of cytoskeletal tension.40 During this process, fibroblasts 
also start expressing cytoskeletal machinery necessary for contrac-
tile functions, including α-­smooth muscle actin (α-­SMA) and these 
effector cells are referred to as myofibroblasts.41 Myofibroblasts 
generate long-­lived isometric contractions via a so-­called lockstep 
mechanism that requires continual synthesis and remodeling of the 
ECM to sustain pericellular tension.42 This biomechanical signaling, 
via tissue “stiffness,” is now recognized as a pivotal mechanism for 
promoting and sustaining the contractile myofibroblast phenotype. 
One of the major drivers of this polarization is TGF-­β.2,39,41 The func-
tional polarization of myofibroblasts also influences the phenotype 
of neighboring cells through modification of the ECM. Alteration of 
matrix properties by the action of cross-­linking enzymes and extra-
cellular peroxidases also generates a positive feedback loop to in-
crease fibroblast contractility and ECM stiffness.43-­45 The power of 
this fibroblast phenotype and regulatory loop is abundantly clear, as 
dysregulation drives the progression of diseases such as fibrosis and 
cancer.31,46
There are several notable examples of fibroblasts performing 
immune-­modulatory functions that are the result of functional 
polarization. Within lymphoid tissues, fibroblasts play a role in 
immune tolerance through the presentation of antigens, both 
via classical MHC-­mediated presentation and through MHC-­
independent display of antigens.47 Conversely, fibroblasts activate 
immune responses via MHC-­independent display of antigens to 
B cells in secondary and tertiary lymphoid organs.48 This crucial 
function is performed by follicular dendritic cells, which despite 
their name are of mesenchymal origin. Follicular dendritic cell 
development is dependent on polarizing signals from lymphotox-
ins and TNFα.49 There is also emerging evidence that fibroblasts 
modulate immune responses by more direct mechanisms following 
inflammatory stimuli. In response to IFNγ, FRCs produce the inhib-
itory molecule nitric oxide to limit T cell proliferation within the 
lymph node.50,51 Human FRCs were also found to exert inhibitory 
effects on T cells through indolamine 2,3 dioxygenase, cycloox-
ygenases 1 and 2, and TGF-­β.52 Fibroblasts can also enhance im-
mune responses by producing growth factors and cytokines. For 
example, FRCs can produce IL-­6, enhancing T cell fitness through 
chromatin remodeling.53 Data also suggest that fibroblasts serve 
as sentinels and respond to a number of innate immune stimuli 
through expression of pattern recognition receptors such as Toll-­
like receptors (TLRs).13,54,55 TLR expression in fibroblasts has 
primarily been observed in inflammatory settings, such as viral 
infection in the intestine56 and rheumatoid arthritis.57,58 However, 
the extent to which fibroblasts contribute to pathogen detection 
in the absence of overt inflammation remains to be determined.
Epigenetic regulation downstream of environmental cues can 
also influence fibroblast identity. Similar to macrophages, epigen-
etic marks, such as DNA methylation, have been found to affect 
the downstream programming of fibroblasts. This modulation may 
occur during development or adulthood in response to environ-
mental stimuli. For example, there is evidence that environmental 
stressors are responsible for the variability in the epigenetic land-
scape of dermal fibroblasts in humans.59 In addition, many disease-­
associated changes observed in fibroblasts are thought to be the 
result of epigenetic alterations.60 Furthermore, epigenetic silencing 
may explain the differences in responsiveness to certain cues by fi-
broblasts compared to more “sensitive” macrophages. This height-
ened sensitivity of macrophages will be discussed more in the next 
section. Lastly, the impact of tissue residence time on fibroblast 
phenotype is unknown. Transplantation experiments that would 
address this question (as are used for macrophages) are technologi-
cally challenging due to the difficulty in depleting specific fibroblast 
populations.
3  |  MACROPHAGES: NOT JUST BIG 
EATERS
3.1  |  Core functions
Macrophages are tissue-­resident innate immune cells with diverse 
functions in development, repair, tissue homeostasis, and host 
defense.61,62 While they are best known for their role as immune 
sentinels through pathogen detection and initiation of inflamma-
tory responses, macrophages are now recognized as instrumental 
players in the maintenance of tissue homeostasis.3 The univer-
sal feature of the macrophage was astutely described upon its 
naming as “macro”-­“phage” or “large”-­“eater” by Eli Metchnikov 
FI G U R E 1 Factors affecting fibroblast and macrophage 
identities. Cell-­intrinsic and cell-­extrinsic factors are responsible 
for cellular identities. Fibroblasts are more affected by cell-­
intrinsic factors such as ontogeny and positional information and 
macrophages are more influenced by cell-­extrinsic factors within 
the tissue microenvironment including tissue-­specific signals and 
duration within the tissue. Importantly, these distinctions are not 
absolute, but serve to illustrate the dominant factors shaping these 
two cell types. Figure created using BioRender [Colour figure can 
be viewed at wileyonlinelibrary.com]
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

90 |   
FRANKLIN
in the late 19th century.63 In both developing and mature organ-
isms, macrophages are phagocytic cells, which engulf and break-­
down components of their environment. The broad targets of 
macrophage-­mediated phagocytosis are any unnecessary or un-
wanted constituents of the tissue. Common examples include 
pathogens, dying cells, and ECM degradation products, but in 
some tissues, the targets can be more specific, such as superflu-
ous neuronal connections in the brain64 or surfactant proteins 
in the lung.65 Yet macrophages are much more than phagocytic 
cells. Macrophages are integral members of the tissue architec-
ture and perform diverse functions due to their proficient sen-
sory capacity. In addition to their role as professional phagocytes, 
macrophages are highly adept sensory cells, detecting a variety of 
cues and stimuli from their environment. This can be observed in 
their capacity to detect pathogens and tissue damage, but also in 
their detection of perturbations in homeostasis and response to 
tissue-­specific environmental signals that instruct their function 
(Figure 1).3
It is well appreciated that macrophages constitute a heteroge-
neous population in both phenotype and function.66 This is best ex-
emplified by the growing list of specialized functions performed by 
macrophages depending on the tissue in which they reside. Examples 
include synaptic pruning and remodeling in the brain,67-­69 mitochon-
drial homeostasis in the heart,70 surfactant clearance in the lung,71 
iron turnover in the spleen,72 and peristalsis in the small intestine.73 
These diverse functions are regulated by distinct transcriptional 
and epigenetic programs, the details of which are currently being 
elucidated. Like fibroblasts, macrophages can also be defined as a 
“supportive” cells within tissues.24 Macrophages perform a number 
of important accessory roles to support proper tissue functioning. 
Again using the lung as an example, alveolar macrophages support 
respiration and gas exchange by sensing surfactant levels and clear-
ing excess surfactant from alveoli through phagocytosis.65
3.2  |  Cellular dynamics
For many decades, it was thought that all tissue macrophage popula-
tions arise from a singular source: hematopoietic stem cell (HSC)-­
derived precursors in the bone marrow. These short-­lived circulating 
precursors, blood monocytes, were thought to be a consistent source 
of all tissue-­resident macrophage populations, extravasating into tis-
sues and differentiating into mature macrophages when necessary. 
While this is partially true in some tissues and contexts, the story is 
more complex. In recent years, this single-­origin paradigm has been 
replaced by a model in which macrophages originate from distinct 
and successive waves of embryonically-­derived and HSC-­derived 
precursors. Embryonic precursors arise from progenitors from both 
the yolk sac and fetal liver. The details of this early seeding during 
development and replacement over time are still debated and dis-
cussed extensively elsewhere.74-­76
Depending on the subset within a given tissue, macrophages can 
either be maintained through in situ proliferation and self-­renewal, 
or via input from circulating monocytes.77 The signals that deter-
mine which route of replenishment dominate within a particular tis-
sue are still unknown. For some locations, like the brain, anatomical 
properties of the tissue can explain the source of replacement. In 
this extreme example, the establishment of the blood-­brain bar-
rier restricts subsequent waves of replenishment, both during de-
velopment and adulthood.75 However, most other tissues exhibit 
heterogeneity when it comes to maintenance of macrophage pop-
ulations. For most of the organs examined in detail, populations 
of both fetal-­derived, self-­renewing macrophages, and monocyte-­
derived macrophages co-­exist, often with distinct anatomical niches 
and functions.78 This heterogeneity has been described in several 
organ systems such as the skin,79 intestine,80 heart,81 and lung.82 
Furthermore, the relative contributions of these different macro-
phage sources change over time, with older animals comprised of 
higher percentages of monocyte-­derived macrophages.82
During inflammation, however, these normal routes of macro-
phage population maintenance are dominated by a massive influx 
of monocytes. This recruitment can occur in all tissue types and is 
not restricted to tissues that already receive input from circulating 
precursors. Recruitment of monocytes occurs quickly and is one of 
the earliest events downstream of pathogen detection and the in-
flammatory cascade.83,84 This dramatic influx of precursors stands in 
contrast to fibroblast populations, which may increase during inflam-
mation, but not on the same scale as macrophages. Alternatively, 
certain stimuli can promote skewing to proliferation of resident mac-
rophage populations over monocyte recruitment.85-­88
Once within tissues, macrophages can also be highly dynamic, 
often showing local migration and extension of cellular processes 
to sense their environment.89 However, the degree of migration 
depends on the particular organ and state of the tissue, as many 
macrophage populations (ie, dermal macrophages) tend to be less 
migratory.90 In this regard, some tissue-­resident macrophage popu-
lations are more similar to sessile fibroblasts at steady state.91 The 
length of time that inflammatory monocyte-­derived macrophages 
survive within tissues is still under investigation. Currently, it seems 
that in some tissues and conditions, these inflammatory populations 
can persist long-­term within tissues, influencing the state and func-
tion of the tissue.92 Lineage tracing and imaging technologies will 
address these open questions.
3.3  |  Determinants of cellular function
One of the key features of macrophages is their phenotypic speci-
ficity depending on the tissue in which they reside.93 This raises 
the question: how do they know which tissue-­specific functions 
to perform? Macrophages are particularly sensitive to changes in 
their environment (Figure 1). This is due, at least in part, to their 
expression of a broad range of receptors that detect pathogens, 
tissue damage, cytokines, and other signaling molecules.94,95 It is 
also possible that increased macrophage sensitivity is due to differ-
ential tuning of receptors between fibroblasts and macrophages, 
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

    |  91
FRANKLIN
but this has not been demonstrated. The efficiency at which mac-
rophages undergo functional polarization in response to tissue-­
specific signals is particularly important due to their replacement 
by infiltrating precursors in certain tissues and situations.77 In 
order for recruited monocytes to perform their tissue-­specific 
functions as fully differentiated macrophages, they must be highly 
responsive to the microenvironment.
Data over the past several years suggest that environmental 
cues play a large role in imprinting macrophage identity in different 
anatomical niches.93 These environmental signals activate master 
transcription factors controlling tissue-­specific functions. Many of 
the transcription factors that are important for the programming 
of macrophage populations have been identified.96 These include 
core transcription factors that are common to all macrophage 
populations and play a role in macrophage differentiation, such 
as PU.1, MYB, C-­MAF, MAFB, and ZEB2, as well as tissue-­specific 
transcription factors such as BACH1 and SPIC for red pulp macro-
phages72,97 and GATA6 for peritoneal macrophages.94,98,99 A few 
known examples of environmental signals that drive expression 
of these transcription factors and polarization of tissue-­resident 
macrophage populations include retinoic acid for cavity macro-
phages98,100 and heme and IL-­33 for red pulp macrophages.97,101 
Importantly, these polarization signals can only induce functional 
polarization in competent cells. For example, precursors of peri-
toneal macrophages, but not progenitors of bone marrow macro-
phages, express the transcription factor GATA6 in the presence 
of retinoic acid.98 The importance of a “competent” cell state that 
allows for specific polarization highlights the role of ontogeny and 
epigenetic influences on macrophage identity.96 Environmental 
signals drive the selection of specific enhancers, adding another 
layer of complexity to the regulation of tissue-­resident macrophage 
identity.102,103 Indeed, a study comparing the enhancer landscapes 
of microglia and large peritoneal macrophages revealed that 60% 
of the active enhancers are shared, but 40% are macrophage 
subset-­specific.102 Elegant lineage tracing, depletion, and trans-
plantation experiments demonstrate that monocyte-­derived mac-
rophages can replace embryonically derived macrophages within 
tissues and perform many of the same functions.104,105 However, 
there are aspects of the macrophage transcriptional program that 
a newly recruited cell does not inherit. For instance, while mono-
cytes can replace Kupffer cells in the liver, their transcriptional 
and functional identities are still distinguishable from Kupffer cells 
seeded during development.106 Furthermore, mature peritoneal 
or liver macrophages were unable to give rise to alveolar macro-
phages in neonatal mice.105 These findings may be explained by 
cell-­intrinsic factors, such as ontogeny, or by the epigenetic his-
tory of the transplanted cells.
A final factor influencing macrophage identity is duration 
within a given tissue.37 Phenotypically and transcriptionally dis-
tinct macrophage populations of different origins and residence 
times can be found alongside each other in the same tissue. 
This is reflective of the distinct dynamics of replenishment and 
lifespans between macrophage populations.80,107-­109 Aside from 
complicating our understanding of macrophage biology, what ef-
fect does this have on macrophage function? As was mentioned 
above, in addition to discrete gene expression patterns, these cell 
populations exhibit unique patterns of open chromatin, suggesting 
some limit to the functional outputs and plasticity of these cells. 
Guilliams and Svedberg propose that residency time determines 
the chromatin state and thus the degree of functional plasticity 
of macrophages.92 They further hypothesize that in the steady 
state, tissue-­resident macrophages display epigenetic silencing of 
inflammatory genes, limiting their potential to inflict inappropriate 
damage on host tissues during an immune response.92 In contrast, 
when inflammatory monocytes are recruited to an inflamed tissue, 
a lack of silencing would allow induction of necessary inflamma-
tory genes to manage the pathogenic threat that recruited them. 
However, if these cells were to reside long-­term in the tissue fol-
lowing resolution, perhaps they also would also undergo silenc-
ing and show reduced capacity to induce inflammatory genes.92 
Future studies will define the parameters of this model. The above 
discussion leaves us with an important question: where do these 
environmental signals come from? Fibroblasts are often, but not 
always, the source of these critical cues. In later sections, the ben-
efits of macrophage-­fibroblast crosstalk in maintaining tissue ho-
meostasis will be discussed.
4  |  FIBROBLASTS AND MACROPHAGES: 
CELL TYPES WITH COMPLEMENTARY 
FUNCTIONS
As is evident from the earlier discussion, the most fundamen-
tal features of macrophages and fibroblasts are quite distinct. 
Fibroblasts provide structure to tissues through the deposition 
of ECM components. In contrast, macrophages breakdown and 
remove components from the extracellular environment through 
phagocytosis. In other words, fibroblasts “make stuff” and mac-
rophages “eat stuff.” At its core, these two functions are highly 
complementary, providing the first hint that that these two uni-
versal cell types are well-­equipped to be partners in maintain-
ing tissue structure and function. In the following sections, the 
complementarity of macrophage and fibroblast functions will dis-
cussed with the remodeling of ECM and production of chemokines 
and growth factors serving as examples.
4.1  |  Extracellular matrix
ECM remodeling, which includes both deposition and degradation 
of ECM components, illustrates two complementary functions of 
fibroblasts and macrophages. In order for tissues to function, the 
appropriate tissue structure must be maintained. This “quantity” 
and “quality” of ECM include stiffness, density, and composition. 
To this end, fibroblasts are required for the deposition of ECM 
components, in a manner specific to each tissue type. This can 
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

92 |   
FRANKLIN
be observed by the different degrees of stiffness that are found 
in distinct tissues.110 Furthermore, the individual constituents of 
the ECM also differ between tissues. For example, human bone is 
composed mostly of collagen I, whereas skeletal muscle is com-
prised of a combination of collagen I, collagen III, versican, elastin, 
and fibronectin.111
In addition, the ECM is not an inert structure and is continuously 
remodeled.6 In order for the ECM to adapt to environmental changes, 
it must be modified, broken down, and rebuilt. Macrophages play a 
key role in this remodeling process, by degrading ECM through the 
production of proteases, cathepsins, and other molecules.112 This is 
particularly apparent during inflammation when the breakdown of 
ECM is required to facilitate leukocyte entry into sites of infection 
or damage. For instance, macrophages increase production of matrix 
metalloproteinase (MMP)-­2 and MMP-­9 in response to inflammatory 
cytokines such as TNFα and IL-­1β.113 Under homeostatic conditions, 
the contribution of macrophages to remodeling has also been ob-
served in a variety of tissues. Perhaps, the best example is the bone. 
Osteoclasts, or specialized macrophages, and osteoblasts, special-
ized fibroblasts, are responsible for the breakdown and rebuilding 
of bone throughout the lifespan of vertebrates. Osteoclasts break-
down and resorb bone and osteoblasts deposit ECM that forms 
the specific bone structure.114 Without these opposing functions 
provided by these two cell types, the bone would not be a dynamic 
and regenerative tissue providing structure, support, and organ pro-
tection to vertebrates. Evidence of macrophage-­mediated remod-
eling of ECM has also been observed in the nulliparous mammary 
gland. Following macrophage depletion in mice, increased levels of 
hyaluronan and collagen were found within the mammary gland, 
demonstrating a role for macrophages in degradation and clearance 
of specific ECM components.115 Dysregulation of these processes 
can be a major driver of diseases including fibrosis and cancer.116
The complementarity and joint regulation of ECM by fibroblasts 
and macrophages extend to additional physiological processes, 
such as tissue repair and regeneration.117 In response to injury, an 
inflammatory response is initiated, largely due to the sensory capac-
ity of macrophages.113 First, the coagulation cascade is activated, 
triggering platelet aggregation, clot formation, and deposition of 
ECM. Concurrently, indicators of cellular damage such as damage-­
associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) are detected by macro-
phages, triggering an inflammatory response. This is characterized 
by the recruitment of monocytes and neutrophils to debride the 
wound. Fibroblasts are then polarized into myofibroblasts through 
wound-­healing cytokines including PDGF, TGF-­β, and IL-­13.28 These 
mediators transform local and recruited fibroblasts into α-­SMA-­
expressing and ECM-­producing myofibroblasts. Myofibroblasts 
promote wound contraction, using their contractile properties to 
pull the edges of the wound toward the center. They also secrete 
mitogenic and chemotactic factors for epithelial and endothelial 
cells, which grow inward, forming new ECM and blood vessels as 
they migrate toward the center of the wound. At each of these steps, 
remodeling of the ECM must occur and involves deposition of ECM 
components by fibroblasts and degradation of ECM and removal of 
damaged cells by macrophages.118 Importantly, the secretory and 
mechanical aspects of tissue repair rely on essential contributions 
from both fibroblasts and macrophages. Depletion of macrophages 
at different phases of skin repair results in impaired healing and 
resolution, including reduced formation of vascularized granulation 
tissue and decreased epithelialization.119 Macrophages are also re-
quired for collagen fibril assembly following injury, demonstrating 
that in addition to secretion of ECM components and remodeling en-
zymes, macrophages also regulate the 3-­D organization and size of 
ECM structures.120 Likewise, deletion of fibroblasts within the fascia 
of deep healing wounds results in prolonged open wounds due to 
the lack of newly synthesized and deposited matrix.121
While the examples provided above fit this simplified model, 
it is important to note that there are several situations, especially 
during inflammation, where the roles of macrophages and fibro-
blasts in ECM deposition and degradation become less distinct. For 
example, in the synovium of human patients with rheumatoid arthri-
tis, fibroblasts play a significant role in the breakdown of ECM.122 
Furthermore, in tissue regeneration, macrophages can contribute to 
the deposition of ECM components such as collagen.123
4.2  |  Chemokines
Chemokines, or chemoattractant cytokines, recruit cells to par-
ticular anatomical locations. Macrophages are major producers of 
chemokines, recruiting innate and adaptive immune cells to sites 
of infection or damage. More recently, it was discovered that fibro-
blasts also play an important role in chemokine secretion both dur-
ing steady state and in response to inflammation.124-­126 While both 
of these cell types produce chemokines, closer inspection suggests 
that the kinds of chemokines that are made, and when, are distinct 
and often have complementary functions. Very broadly, fibroblasts 
appear to act more as constitutive signposts, marking a given geo-
graphical location. Chemokines are produced to recruit or maintain 
populations in a given niche or regulate the organization of immune 
cell trafficking. Macrophages, however, produce chemokines on de-
mand, at a time or place that cells are needed. Production is usually 
in response to macrophage sensing of infection, damage, or disrup-
tions in homeostasis and can include CXCL1, CXCL2, CXCL8, CCL2, 
CCL3, CCL4, and CCL5.127 This inflammatory response activated by 
macrophages and other immune cells is then further perpetuated 
by chemokine production by fibroblasts and other non-­immune cell 
types within inflamed tissues.
Fibroblasts are a major source of chemokines that are produced 
constitutively at steady state and are required for a number of ho-
meostatic process in lymphoid organs such as the bone marrow, 
lymph nodes, and spleen. For example, CCL19 and CCL21 are pro-
duced by mesenchymal cells in the T cell zones of lymph nodes and 
spleen and regulate the homing of lymphocytes and myeloid cells to 
spatially segregated compartments within secondary lymphoid tis-
sues.16 This facilitates cognate antigen-­specific interactions within 
lymphoid tissues, resulting in the generation of adaptive immune 
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

    |  93
FRANKLIN
responses. CXCL13 is produced by follicular dendritic cells, a type 
of specialized fibroblastic cell in secondary lymphoid tissues, and is 
critical for germinal center formation and efficient trafficking of B 
cells and follicular CD4 helper T cells to and within lymphoid folli-
cles.128-­130 CCL19, CCL21, and CXCL13 are also expressed by stro-
mal cells in gut-­associated lymphoid tissue (GALT), including Peyer's 
patches.125 There, these chemokines are required for both the at-
traction and retention of lymphoid tissue inducer cells in develop-
ing Peyer's patches and for organized lymphocyte migration and 
dendritic cell adhesion during immune responses.131 Another ho-
meostatic chemokine, CXCL12, is produced by fibroblastic cells of 
the bone marrow niche, mesenchymal stem and progenitor cells.132 
These bone marrow niches are not only important for hematopoietic 
stem cell quiescence and differentiation, but also for the develop-
ment of T and B cells.133-­135 For this reason, CXCL12 can also be 
considered a growth factor and is discussed in more detail in the 
following section.
Importantly, this distinction between fibroblast production of 
constitutive chemokines and macrophage production of chemo-
kines on demand is less suitable under inflammatory conditions. 
In response to inflammatory cues, fibroblasts make a number of 
chemokines (and other cytokines) that act to recruit and polarize 
immune cells. This is particularly evident in the setting of tumor 
growth, where cancer-­associated fibroblasts upregulate a number of 
tissue-­modulatory factors including chemokines.4,136 However, de-
spite these functional changes that occur during inflammation, the 
distinct role for fibroblasts and macrophages in chemokine produc-
tion can be useful when considering the homeostatic functions of 
these two cell types.
4.3  |  Growth factors
Growth factors are required for the survival of normal (untrans-
formed) cells.137 They are produced by a variety of different cell 
types in both homeostatic and inflammatory conditions. When 
considering the growth factors produced by macrophages and fi-
broblasts, patterns emerge under homeostatic conditions. During 
steady state, fibroblasts and macrophages make a selection of 
growth factors that are quite different in nature. Macrophages pro-
duce factors for a variety of fully differentiated cells within tissues 
such as epithelial and parenchymal cells (IGF1),138 endothelial cells 
(VEGFA & VEGFC),139,140 and fibroblasts (PDGF).138,141 In contrast, 
fibroblasts produce a variety of growth factors for progenitor cells, 
acting as important regulators of stem cell or progenitor cell niches. 
In the bone marrow, mesenchymal stromal cells produce CXCL12 to 
promote hematopoietic stem cell maintenance.142 In addition, these 
same mesenchymal cells, also referred to as mesenchymal stem 
and progenitor cells, support a number of lymphoid precursors in 
the bone marrow through production of CXCL12, IL-­7, and IL-­15.132 
There is also recent evidence that these same cells produce factors 
necessary for the survival of myeloid progenitors, including CSF1, 
IL-­34, and FLT3L.143
While this generalization is one way to structure our think-
ing about the different roles of macrophages and fibroblasts 
in growth factor production, it is incomplete. An important ex-
ception to this framework exists in regards to some mature im-
mune cell populations. For instance, fibroblasts produce the 
macrophage-­specific growth factor CSF1 both in vitro138 and in 
vivo.144,145 CSF1 production can be for both fully-­differentiated 
macrophages, or for hematopoietic stem cell-­derived macro-
phage precursors (monocytes). In addition, fibroblasts (FRCs 
specifically) produce IL-­7 for fully mature, yet naïve, T cells in sec-
ondary lymphoid tissues.146 As is exhibited by these examples, 
determining an underlying theme for the types of growth factors 
made by fibroblasts and macrophages is much more complicated 
than a simple binary distinction.
The boundaries of this generalization also change under extreme 
perturbations. During inflammation, macrophages induce growth 
factors as an emergency function to clear pathogens and promote 
tissue repair. Under these conditions, in both mice and humans, mac-
rophages have been found to both increase production of growth 
factors expressed during homeostasis such as VEGF,147 IGF1,30,148 
and PDGF,30,149,150 while also diversifying their growth factor rep-
ertoire. For example, macrophages in the lung produce Wnt ligands 
for epithelial progenitors in response to both sterile and non-­sterile 
damage to help drive differentiation and regeneration of infected 
or damaged tissues.151 Similarly, in a model of liver injury, macro-
phages induce WNT3A, which enhances the specification of hepatic 
progenitor cells to mature hepatocytes.152 Interestingly in this ex-
ample, fibroblasts are a source of Notch ligand, which specifies an 
alternate fate of bipotent hepatic progenitor cells away from mature 
hepatocytes, toward cholangiocytes.152 This example suggests the 
partnership between fibroblasts and macrophages in local niches 
may help determine fate choice between two alternative fates, 
pointing to an even more complex coordination between these two 
cell types. These examples also provide evidence that growth factor 
expression in fibroblasts and macrophages is responsive to changing 
environmental cues.
4.4  |  United by differences: cellular heterogeneity
In addition to engaging in these complementary functions, fibro-
blasts and macrophages share an important feature that allows for 
diverse interactions, cellular heterogeneity. It may seem like an ob-
vious or trivial statement, but the heterogeneity shared by these 
two fundamental cell types points both to the complexity of their 
interactions and importance of tissue-­specific signals to instruct 
their function (as discussed previously). Heterogeneity comes at 
the level of inter-­tissue differences, but also at the intra-­tissue 
level. In fact, it has been observed that both fibroblasts and mac-
rophages exhibit heterogeneity in different regions of the same tis-
sue. Fibroblast heterogeneity has been observed in lymph nodes,153 
intestine,154,155 dermis,156 lung,157 and many other tissues in both 
mice and humans. These findings also extend to macrophages, as 
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

94 |   
FRANKLIN
discussed above. It remains to be seen whether the same local sig-
nals can have complementary effects on both macrophages and 
fibroblasts. For example, in tissues where a specific signal is impor-
tant for the polarization of macrophages, such as heme for red pulp 
macrophages, does that same signal have an effect on fibroblasts? 
Do these tissue-­specific signals specify a master program impor-
tant for homeostasis of that particular organ? If true, however, it 
is important to note that in some tissues, this may be less relevant 
because fibroblasts are the source of the polarizing signal and do 
not respond to it directly themselves.
The examples above identify potential distinctions and parallels 
between fibroblasts and macrophages. There is abundant evidence 
that these two cell types interact within tissues, but the benefits 
of this communication have not yet been discussed in detail. The 
following section will examine the general advantages of two cells 
working together to maintain tissue homeostasis and why fibro-
blasts and macrophages are well-­suited for this role.
5  |  TWO IS BETTER THAN ONE
In complex organisms, different cell types evolved to execute dis-
tinct functions. This is observed in the earliest metazoan tissues, like 
those of Placozoa, Ctenophora, Porifera, and Cnidaria, which consist 
of epithelial and mesenchymal cells.158,159 In this basic tissue, epithe-
lial cells create a barrier separating the internal environment from 
the external environment and the mesenchymal cells provide the tis-
sue with structural integrity and organization.24 This basic function 
of mesenchymal cells described here mirrors the earlier discussion of 
fibroblasts as structural cells. Clear divisions of labor became more 
complex as organisms evolved more specialized organs and tissue 
types.160 In all of these contexts, communication between different 
cell types is important to coordinate tissue and organismal func-
tions. Within tissues, a cell circuit has benefits over two cells act-
ing in isolation; cell circuits allow for (a) control of cell population 
sizes and geographical organization relative to one another and (b) 
a wider range of strategies to regulate/tune/coordinate functional 
responses to stimuli.
These two key benefits of cell-­cell communication circuits pro-
vide a framework to consider the potential outcomes of two inter-
acting cells. Cell circuits can be classified as “organizational” and/or 
“functional” circuits (Figure 2). Organizational circuits act primarily 
to control cell composition within a tissue, while functional circuits 
are engaged to regulate and coordinate functions in response to 
stimuli. Importantly, these two categories are not mutually exclusive 
but rather serve as a way to emphasize the downstream output of 
a given communication circuit. Both circuits can respond to exter-
nal signals and demands; however, the end goal of these different 
outputs is different. In other words, describing circuits as members 
of one or both of these two broad categories provides a framework 
to consider the downstream effect or purpose of a given cell-­cell 
interaction. Furthermore, fibroblasts and macrophages are two cell 
types that engage in both of these circuit types and can be used to 
understand how this framework may be applied to other pairs of 
interacting cells.
Organizational circuits control cell numbers, ratios, and geo-
graphical location of cells within a tissue. The main purpose of these 
circuits is to maintain the function of a given tissue. For example, in 
the spleen, WT1+ reticular fibroblasts dictate the number and loca-
tion of red pulp macrophages through the production of CSF1.144 
Without the presence of red pulp macrophages, the spleen can 
longer perform one of its key functions, iron recycling. Another 
example of an organizational circuit in vivo is between fibroblasts 
and macrophages in the lymph node. RANKL expression by mar-
ginal reticular cells is required for proper localization of subcapsular 
sinus macrophages.161,162 Subcapsular sinus macrophages form the 
first line of defense in the lymph node by capturing lymph-­borne 
pathogens and preventing them from entering the parenchyma of 
the lymph node.163
Organizational circuits often work through growth factors, 
as one of the main ways to control cell number within a tissue is 
through the production of growth factors.138,164 These circuits are 
operational both during homeostatic and inflammatory conditions. 
During homeostasis, organizational circuits ensure stable cell popu-
lations in the correct location within tissues. In the context of inflam-
mation, these circuits can be dialed up to allow transient increases in 
FI G U R E 2 Organizational and functional circuits. Cellular circuits can be considered as organizational and/or functional depending 
on particular features. Organizational circuits control cell composition within a tissue and functional circuits regulate and coordinate 
functional responses to stimuli. These two categories are not mutually exclusive, but emphasize the downstream output of a given 
communication circuit. In addition, both circuits can respond to external signals and demands; however, the outputs of these circuits are 
different: composition vs. response. The output of the organizational circuit depicted here is maintenance of stable cell ratios through 
reciprocal exchange of growth factors. The output of the functional circuit is the polarization of fibroblasts into myofibroblasts in response 
to paracrine signaling from macrophages activated by inflammatory cues. Figure created using BioRender [Colour figure can be viewed at 
wileyonlinelibrary.com]

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

    |  95
FRANKLIN
a given population. For example, in the bone at homeostasis, CSF1 
and RANKL are provided to osteoclasts to promote their survival.114 
However, during inflammation, inflammatory signals such as TNF 
can act on osteoblasts to produce more RANKL, thus increasing the 
number of osteoclasts.165 Another general feature of organizational 
circuits is that the phenotype or function of the cells in the circuit 
typically remains the same, instead, the number or location of the 
cells will change.
Functional circuits represent the other key benefit of cell-­cell cir-
cuitry: the ability for cells to amplify, inhibit, or coordinate their re-
sponses to a given stimuli. These cell-­cell interactions allow for highly 
adjustable responses to the external environment that would not be 
possible if one cell acted alone. Functional circuits operate in two ways: 
(a) one cell type can sense a given environmental stimulus or (b) both 
cell types can sense the stimulus. When only one cell type in the circuit 
can sense the stimulus, the other cell type receives information about 
the stimulus through paracrine signaling from the stimulus-­sensing cell. 
This indirect mode of sensing is best demonstrated through the logic of 
the homeostatic circuit.24 In the homeostatic circuit, one cell type (the 
“sensor”) detects an external stimulus or demand from the tissue and 
communicates this stimulus through a paracrine signal to another cell 
type (the “effector”). The effector is then responsible for resolving the 
need or demand within the tissue. In the case of hypoxia, tissue-­resident 
macrophages detect low oxygen levels and respond by secreting VEGF 
and other angiogenic signals.140,166 This paracrine signal acts on endo-
thelial cells, which are the effectors in this circuit. The outcome of this 
interaction is local angiogenesis and increased oxygenation of the tissue, 
thus correcting the hypoxic environment. These are changes that would 
not occur without the presence of both cells in the circuit. Importantly, 
activation of functional circuits by external stimuli results in phenotypic 
changes in one or more of the cells in the circuit. This is in contrast to 
organizational circuits which generally do not result in phenotypic 
changes in individual cells. In a similar fashion as organizational circuits, 
functional circuits are active both during homeostasis and inflammation.
An example of a functional circuit is the production of TGF-­β by 
macrophages in response to inflammatory cues such as TNFα and 
IFNγ (Figure  2). TGF-­β is well-­characterized to induce phenotypic 
changes in fibroblasts and polarization to myofibroblasts as discussed 
in detail previously.39 Sensing of TGF-­β by myofibroblasts enhances 
ECM deposition and proliferation, driving tissue repair. In functional 
circuits where both cell types can sense the stimuli, existence of 
cell-­cell communication can also allow for coordinated responses or 
tuning of a given response.24 Fibroblasts and macrophages engage in 
both organizational and functional circuits. In the following section, 
a recently described organizational circuit between these two cell 
types in vitro will be discussed in more detail.138,164
6  |  THE FIBROBLAST-­MACROPHAGE 
CIRCUIT
The two-­cell circuit formed between fibroblasts and macrophages in 
vitro represents an example of an organizational circuit. It was found 
that these two cell types engage in a stable interaction to control rela-
tive cell ratios through the reciprocal exchange of growth factors.138,164 
By using a reductionist approach, the key features required for this sta-
bility were uncovered and it is likely that these features are generaliza-
ble to fibroblast-­macrophage interactions in vivo as well as interactions 
between additional cell types. These studies revealed three circuit fea-
tures important for stability; (a) one cell type close to the limit imposed 
by external constraints, also known as carrying capacity, (b) negative 
regulation, and (c) cell-­cell contact.138,164
Carrying capacity is defined as the maximum population size that 
can be supported in a given environment.167 It is determined by ex-
trinsic factors, such as oxygen, nutrients, or space. When available 
resources are not limiting, self-­replicating populations can undergo 
periods of exponential growth, but as the population size increases, 
some environmental factors become limiting and the growth rate 
declines to zero.168 In the in vitro system used in this study, carry-
ing capacity is defined as the cell number where the proliferation rate 
is zero. We found that fibroblasts are stable close to their carrying 
capacity while macrophages are stable far from their carrying capac-
ity.138,164 This allows macrophages to expand on demand, mirroring 
the transient expansion that occurs during inflammation. This general 
principle is likely used by other cell-­cell partnerships where it is bene-
ficial for cells to expand or shrink depending on environmental signals.
Negative regulation is another feature required for circuit sta-
bility.138,164 In this system, negative regulation controls the mac-
rophage population, the cell type that is not limited by carrying 
capacity. This feedback can occur through auto-­repression or cross-­
repression, with each design generating different circuit topolo-
gies. The observed circuit comprised a negative signal in the form 
of auto-­repression by receptor endocytosis-­mediated growth factor 
depletion. This internalization mechanism is more desirable relative 
to cross-­regulation, such as through changes in growth factor pro-
duction, because it results in a circuit more robust to parameter vari-
ation that returns to stability more rapidly following perturbation.164
The last feature key to stability between macrophages and fi-
broblasts is cell-­cell contact. Using a co-­culture system with a mixed 
population of fibroblasts containing both fibroblasts that expressed 
the macrophage-­specific growth factor CSF1 and fibroblasts that did 
not, we found that only CSF1-­producing fibroblasts were in contact 
with macrophages.138 Furthermore, both growth factor-­sufficient 
cell types (fibroblasts and macrophages) exhibited a growth advan-
tage. In contrast, the fibroblasts that were deficient for CSF1 were 
depleted from the co-­culture over time. While cell contact proved 
to be important in this setting, it is unknown what mediates this 
contact-­dependent mechanism. The following section will discuss 
the benefits of cell contact and the potential mechanisms by which 
two cell types come together.
7  |  CELLS IN CONTACT
The above findings bring attention to an important question when 
thinking about cell-­cell interactions, why is contact important? In 
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

96 |   
FRANKLIN
the case of secreted proteins or other signals, one explanation is 
that by being in close contact, the concentration of cells that are 
necessary within a given space is decreased. Data from our study 
support this hypothesis.138,164 Indeed, we found both experimen-
tally and through mathematical modeling that circuit stability is 
density-­dependent, suggesting that when cells are at low density, 
cell contact decreases the required amount of growth factor for a 
given number of cells.138 It is also possible that contact itself is not 
required in vivo, but only appeared to be essential in the co-­culture 
system. Perhaps local concentrations of growth factor need to reach 
a certain threshold and this is only achieved in the culture conditions 
by cells in contact. In vivo, growth factors may be deposited on ECM 
in such a way that cells do not necessarily need to be in contact, 
just close enough to receive the level of growth factor required for 
survival and proliferation.6 This again highlights the importance of 
quantity and quality of ECM in communication, especially between 
fibroblasts and macrophages.
Cell contact has additional benefits aside from decreasing the 
threshold concentration of secreted proteins. For instance, cell 
contact may facilitate further communication between the two 
cell types, such as the mechanisms observed in the communication 
between macrophages and epithelial cells in the lung via connexin 
43-­containing gap junctions. In this mode of communication, mac-
rophages form junctional channels with the epithelium and estab-
lish intercommunication through synchronized Ca2+ waves, using 
the epithelium as the conducting pathway.169 This pathway was also 
found to connect cardiac macrophages and cardiomyocytes in the 
atrioventricular node, facilitating electrical conduction.170 Another 
advantage of cell-­cell contact between fibroblasts and macrophages 
is the propagation of coordinated responses, promoting the afore-
mentioned functional circuit. Cell contact would also allow for di-
rect feedback if one cell provides growth factors to another cell in 
exchange for receiving its own. This could prevent “cheaters” in the 
system who receive growth factor, but do not produce it. This circuit 
involving reciprocal exchange of growth factors has an additional 
advantage over an autocrine circuit: An extra mode of regulation 
to control cell population size can prevent unwarranted expansion 
within the tissue context. It also provides the circuitry to relay de-
mands of a whole tissue (ie, higher cell numbers) to a specific cell 
type.
While the fibroblast-­macrophage circuit exhibits the minimal cell 
communication circuit required for stability, it is by no means the 
only growth factor circuit to exist between cells. Our studies iden-
tified 48 possible circuits that allow a stable steady state for a wide 
range of parameters.138,164 It is likely that these distinct topologies 
are found for different cells and settings. Furthermore, these cell 
circuit interactions are generalizable to different pairs of interact-
ing cells aside from fibroblasts and macrophages. Several potential 
circuits have been discovered between CSF1-­providing cells and 
tissue-­resident macrophages. For example, lymphatic endothelial 
cells are an important source of CSF1 for subcapsular sinus macro-
phages found in lymph nodes.171 Liver sinusoidal endothelial cells 
and hepatic stellate cells can also produce CSF1 for macrophages 
within the liver.145 Similar circuits have also been described between 
cells in the hematopoietic stem cell niche within the bone mar-
row.133,134 In fact, the finding of growth factor exchange combined 
with the proximity between macrophages and fibroblasts is highly 
reminiscent of the hematopoietic stem cell niche and suggests that 
this niche concept can be applied to many tissue-­resident cell-­cell 
interactions.172
8  |  A MUTUAL ATTRACTION
It is known from both in vitro and in vivo studies that fibroblasts 
and macrophages are often found in close proximity to each other 
and engage in frequent communication,91,138,164 [reviewed by Turley 
and colleagues].173 In several settings, this has been explained by 
exchange of signals, including growth factors, to support one or 
both cell types. However, it seems likely that these cell types are 
attracted to each other in response to additional signals. How do 
macrophages and fibroblasts find each other? The likely candidates 
in fibroblast-­macrophage attraction are growth factors, chemokines, 
and mechanical cues.
8.1  |  Attraction via growth factors
Growth factors are required for the proliferation and survival of 
non-­transformed cells, with growth factor deprivation leading to 
apoptosis.137 In addition to their roles in survival and differentia-
tion, growth factors are also known to function as chemoattract-
ants. CSF1 signaling via CSF-­1R induces rapid membrane ruffling, 
followed by cell spreading and polarization.174,175 This process, in-
volving dynamic reorganization of the actin cytoskeleton and focal 
adhesion, results in rapid macrophage chemotaxis. Members of 
the PDGF family are also chemoattractants, with PDGF-­B regulat-
ing the migration of mesenchymal cells both during development 
and in adult tissues.176,177
8.2  |  Attraction via chemokines
Chemokine secretion is the major mechanism used by immune cells 
to recruit specific populations of cells to tissues. In the splenic red 
pulp, upon red pulp macrophage depletion, WT1+ fibroblasts secrete 
chemokines such as CCL2 and CCL7 to recruit monocytes to the 
open macrophage niche.144 In the liver, Kupffer cell deletion led to 
the production of CCL2, CCL7, and CXCL10 by hepatic stellate cells 
(a key hepatic fibroblast population).145 These are just a few of many 
examples of monocytes homing to tissues where they later differ-
entiate into tissue-­resident macrophages. Additionally, as discussed 
previously, CXCL12 is a chemokine known to be produced by many 
fibroblast subtypes, especially in the bone marrow niche. It is pos-
sible that this signal could also be used to attract macrophages, as 
many macrophage populations express the receptor CXCR4.
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

    |  97
FRANKLIN
8.3  |  Attraction via mechanical signals
There are also several studies demonstrating the importance of 
mechanical signals in the attraction of macrophages to specific re-
gions within tissues. Work from the Hinz group showed that my-
ofibroblast contraction provides cues propagated by the ECM that 
work to attract macrophages.178 In fact, they found that in vitro, 
chemotaxis is dispensable for macrophage to myofibroblast attrac-
tion. Macrophages were able to migrate toward myofibroblasts even 
when chemotactic gradients were eliminated by slow fluid flow.178 
It would be interesting to know if this mechanism only acts in the 
context of increased tissue stiffness, such as in wound healing, or if 
this is a steady-­state mechanism as well. One could imagine that if 
this mechanism works only in some contexts when contact between 
these two cell types needs to be increased, it could override the 
homeostatic mechanisms in place to control macrophage-­fibroblast 
interactions. The authors also suggest that chemotactic gradients 
and mechanical signals may act at different ranges; chemokines at 
short range and deformations in ECM at longer ranges.178 Indeed, 
chemical signals are crucial for macrophage interactions with other 
cell types within the tumor microenvironment,179 suggesting that 
mechanical cues provide yet another method of macrophage recruit-
ment to tissues, in addition to growth factors and chemokines.
9  |  PERSPECTIVES
This review discusses fibroblast-­macrophage interactions in the con-
text of their fundamental features. Macrophages and fibroblasts are 
two functionally distinct cell types with unique, yet complementary 
functions. These two universal cell types play non-­redundant roles 
within tissues, making up a “functional unit.” Both cell types contrib-
ute something distinct to a common function (ie, ECM remodeling) 
which cannot be achieved if either cell type is removed. Fibroblasts 
bring important structural components, most notably the deposition 
of ECM, while macrophages contribute their sensory capacity and 
ability to breakdown elements of the environment. Together, they 
allow for a highly regulatable system to maintain tissue composition, 
structure, mechanical properties, and function. The examples herein 
provide illustrations of these complementary functions and a frame-
work in which to consider them.
9.1  |  New technology
Recent advances in sequencing and computational technologies 
have allowed a better understanding of the diversity and hetero-
geneity of both macrophages and fibroblasts. Novel computational 
approaches to infer or predict cell-­cell communication via gene ex-
pression data are also being developed both in the presence and ab-
sence of disease.13,180-­183 Spatial transcriptomics184 and strategies to 
sequence physically interacting cells185 add additional approaches to 
identify cell communication circuits within tissues.
The development of cell-­specific tools to target tissue-­resident 
macrophage populations has allowed identification of new func-
tions. However, analogous tools are still being generated for fibro-
blast populations. More specific strategies to deplete mesenchymal 
cell populations are still required to fully understand their contribu-
tions to tissue homeostasis and disease. This technological advance-
ment has been hindered by the lack of both specific and universal 
fibroblast markers that can be used to conditionally knock-­out genes 
in particular fibroblast populations. With additional sequencing 
datasets, it is likely that new markers will soon be identified.
9.2  |  Fibroblast-­macrophage interactions over time
With the emerging data on macrophage ontogeny and details of the 
temporal changes in macrophage populations, it is now clear that 
as animals age, tissue compartments that were once embryonically-­
derived become dominated by monocyte-­derived cells.75 This sug-
gests that the interactions between infiltrating monocytes and other 
cells within the cellular neighborhood are extremely important. 
Indeed, studies in the lung showed that alveolar macrophages can 
be replaced successfully by bone marrow-­derived monocytes and 
fetal populations similarly.105 The degree to which these interactions 
remain or change in aging individuals will have implications for the 
treatment of aging-­related diseases. Furthermore, it remains to be 
seen whether fibroblast phenotypes change over time as well, and 
if this is driven by macrophages or by shared environmental factors.
9.3  |  Fibroblast-­macrophage interactions in disease
Fibroblasts and macrophages have been implicated in the progres-
sion of a variety of diseases, including fibrosis, autoimmunity, and 
cancer. As we understand more about the complex interactions 
between these two cell types, it is also becoming apparent that 
dysregulation of homeostatic fibroblast-­macrophage interactions 
contributes to disease development. Successful therapeutic strat-
egies will likely come from further engaging or disrupting these 
interactions. The lack of success of current therapeutics dem-
onstrates that cells can no longer be targeted in isolation, espe-
cially in tissue-­level diseases. An example of this was recently 
demonstrated in the context of fibrosis. Paradoxical results have 
been found in the context of macrophage depletion in fibrosis, 
depending on the stage at which macrophages were depleted. 
Adler and colleagues recently demonstrated, using computational 
approaches, that this discrepancy may be due to the bistability ob-
served in the fibroblast-­macrophage circuit.186 If macrophages are 
removed at an early timepoint, before myofibroblasts reach the 
density at which they can support themselves, the system flows 
to the healing state. In contrast, removing macrophages at a later 
timepoint when myofibroblasts no longer rely on growth factor 
provided by macrophages, fibroblasts rapidly flow to the “cold” fi-
brosis state characterized by high fibroblast numbers and elevated 
 1600065x, 2021, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/imr.12989 by Ulsan National Institute Of, Wiley Online Library on [02/05/2024]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

98 |   
FRANKLIN
levels of ECM.186 This bistability may explain why removal of mac-
rophages enhances healing in some studies, yet accelerates fibro-
sis in others.187
10  |  CONCLUDING REMARKS
Fibroblast-­macrophage interactions are key for tissue integrity and 
function. Fibroblasts provide architectural landmarks and struc-
tural components, while macrophages modulate tissue architecture 
through their dynamic sensory capacity and ability to breakdown 
and eliminate components in the environment. By better under-
standing the complex interactions between these two cell types, 
novel therapeutic strategies can be developed to restore proper 
functioning of tissues in a variety of disease contexts.
