MATERIALS AND METHODS
Preparation of digoxigenin-labeled probes
for in situ hybridization
We generated our own full-length clones of XIF3 and
Xenopus NF-L by reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain
reaction (RT-PCR; Davis et al., 1994) from oligo-dT-
selected RNA that was isolated from spinal cords of juve-
nile Xenopus laevis periodic albino frogs (Hoperskaya,
1975). The full-length clones that we used as templates for
transcription of xeﬁltin and XNIF cRNA probes were pre-
pared previously (Zhao and Szaro, 1997a). The oligonucle-
otide primers used for cloning XIF3 and NF-L ﬂanked
both the coding and the 39-untranslated domains. The
primer sequences were as follows: 59 sense XIF3, TGCT-
CATCATGAGTCACTCTGGTCT;
39
antisense
XIF3,
TCGCTTACAGCTGTCACATGTTTGA; 59 sense NF-L,
CGGCACCATGAGCTCGTATAGTTAC;
39
antisense
NF-L,
CATTCATCAGCTGCTCTCTATTGCC.
The
XL-
PCR kit (Perkin Elmer Applied Biosystems, Foster City,
CA) was used for PCR according to the manufacturer’s
instructions with the following parameters: 30 cycles of
denaturing at 94°C for 1 minute, annealing at 55°C for 1
minute, and extension at 68°C for 3 minutes. The result-
ing products were puriﬁed by agarose gel electrophoresis
and were prepared for blunt-end cloning into the SmaI
site of pSPORT1 (Gibco/BRL Life Technologies, Gaithers-
burg, MD) according to standard methods (Davis et al.,
1994). To verify that we had cloned the intended nIF
proteins, we sequenced 150–200 nucleotides from repre-
sentative clones of each of the two genes. The nucleotide
sequences closely matched published sequences of XIF3
($95%; Sharpe et al., 1989) and Xenopus NF-L ($98%;
Charnas et al., 1992) to a degree that is consistent for
Xenopus genes. In addition, we veriﬁed that restriction
sites of several enzymes (XbaI for NF-L and HindIII and
BfaI for XIF3) that are found in the published sequences
also were present in our clones. A smaller (826 nucleotide)
cRNA probe encompassing only the highly divergent
C-terminal and 39-untranslated domains of XIF3 was also
prepared to further exclude cross hybridization with
mRNAs of other IF proteins, which share much less se-
quence identity in these domains than in the rod domain.
This probe was used untruncated.
Plasmids were propagated in DH5a cells and prepared
by using Qiagen maxiprep columns (Qiagen, Stanford,
CA) according to directions of the manufacturer. All plas-
mid templates were linearized in the poly-linker region
with appropriate restriction enzymes producing 59 over-
hangs (New England BioLabs, Beverly, MA). In vitro tran-
scription using SP6 or T7 RNA polymerase and a Genius
4 kit (Roche Molecular Biochemicals, Indianapolis, IN)
yielded digoxigenin-labeled antisense cRNAs of similar
lengths (2.1 kb for xeﬁltin, XNIF, and Xenopus NF-L; 2.0
kb for XIF3) and comparable levels of activity. For a neg-
ative control, sense cRNA was transcribed from a plasmid
containing an XIF3 insert in the sense orientation. After
transcription, cRNA probes used for in situ hybridization
on sections were chemically digested in part into '300
nucleotide fragments to improve penetration (Angerer et
al., 1987). The quality and size of cRNA probes was veri-
ﬁed on formaldehyde/agarose gels (Davis et al., 1994), and
their speciﬁc activities after labeling were compared with
that of a control RNA in a dot-blot assay (Genius 3 kit;
Roche Molecular Biochemicals) to ensure sure that equiv-
alent amounts of probe were used for each experiment.
Tissue preparation
Periodic albino Xenopus laevis that were bred in our
laboratory were fully anesthetized by immersion in 0.1%
3-aminobenzoic acid ethyl ester (Sigma, St. Louis, MO)
according to protocols approved by our Institutional Ani-
mal Care and Use Committee and conforming to National
Institutes of Health guidelines. Embryos and tadpoles up
to stage 40 (2.5-day-old swimming tadpole; Nieuwkoop
and Faber, 1956) were ﬁxed by immersion in a buffer
containing 0.1 M 3-[N-morpholino] propanesulfonic acid,
pH 7.4; 2 mM EGTA; 1 mM MgSO4, and 3.7% formalde-
hyde (MEMFA buffer) from 30 minutes to 2 hours. They
were then washed and stored in 100% methanol at 4°C
until they were processed for wholemount in situ hybrid-
ization. Stage 42 (3-day-old swimming) tadpoles were
ﬁxed by immersion in 3.7% formaldehyde in 0.1 M phos-
phate buffer, pH 7.4 (PB) overnight, embedded in poly-
acrylamide (Hausen and Dreyer, 1981), and frozen for
sectioning. Stage 51 (17-day-old) tadpoles were ﬁxed by
immersion in the same ﬁxative for several days, then
cryoprotected in 30% sucrose in PB for several days, em-
bedded in optimum cutting temperature (OCT) compound
(Miles Inc., Elkhart, IN), and frozen. Juvenile (,3 months
postmetamorphosis) and 7-year-old adult frogs were ﬁxed
by intracardial perfusion with 3.7% paraformaldehyde/
10% sucrose in amphibian Ringer’s solution (0.65% NaCl,
0.042% KCl, 0.025% CaCl2). Heads and torsos of juvenile
frogs and brains and spinal cords of adult frogs were
postﬁxed in the same ﬁxative and then cryoprotected in
25% sucrose/3% polyethylene glycol in amphibian Ringer’s
solution. Tissues were then embedded in OCT compound
and frozen. Frozen sections were cut transversely at 12
mm and thaw mounted onto glass slides treated with poly-
lysine or aminoalkylsilane, as described previously (Ger-
vasi and Szaro, 1995).
In situ hybridization
Wholemount in situ hybridization was performed on
embryos and tadpoles at stages 10 (gastrula) to stage 40,
as described fully by Shain and Zuber (1996). After ﬁxa-
tion and pretreatment, embryos were hybridized with
digoxigenin-labeled cRNA. The optimal condition for in
situ hybridization was an overnight hybridization (18
hours) at 60°C, with probes at a concentration of 1–2
mg/ml. Specimens that were hybridized with higher con-
centrations of probe yielded the same patterns and rela-
tive intensity of speciﬁc staining but had increased back-
ground. After hybridization, embryos were washed twice
in 50% formamide/23 standard saline citrate (SSC) at
60°C for 30 minutes each. After the blocking of nonspeciﬁc
binding sites, the digoxigenin signal was visualized with
antidigoxigenin Fab fragments coupled to alkaline phos-
phatase (Genius 3 kit; Roche Molecular Biochemicals).
After the chromogenic reaction was stopped, the embryos
were stored and observed in MEMFA buffer, except for
some that subsequently were cleared in benzyl alcohol/
benzyl benzoate 1:2 (Sigma) to visualize staining in deep,
underlying structures. Some tailbud-stage embryos were
also embedded in polyacrylamide after in situ hybridiza-
tion and then sectioned by cryostat at 12 mm for further
observation.
514
C. GERVASI ET AL.
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

In situ hybridization on sectioned tissue was performed
as described previously (Zhao and Szaro, 1997a), except
that hybridization was done at 55°C, and the high-
stringency wash was in 0.13 SSC at 60°C. This procedure
uses an RNase incubation (20 mg/ml RNase A, 0.5 M NaCl,
0.25 mM ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid, and 10 mM
Tris, pH 8) to reduce background and increase the speci-
ﬁcity of the probe. The digoxigenin signal was detected as
described for the wholemount in situ hybridization stud-
ies.
To ensure faithful comparisons, wholemount embryos
and slides that were to be compared directly were pro-
cessed in parallel. By using wholemount in situ hybridiza-
tion, we analyzed the following numbers of embryos for
the expression of XIF3: stage 18, 5 embryos (1 experi-
ment); stage 24, 21 embryos (3 experiments); stages 28–
32, 26 embryos (5 experiments); stages 33/34–35/36, 44
embryos (8 experiments). Comparable numbers of em-
bryos were analyzed for XNIF expression at these same
stages. We also sectioned 2 embryos each for XIF3 and
XNIF at stages 28–29/30. For xeﬁltin and NF-L, we ana-
lyzed 22 embryos each at stages 33/34–35/36 (3 experi-
ments). For in situ hybridization of sectioned tissue, we
prepared 4 juvenile frog heads and 2 juvenile spinal cords.
One animal each was analyzed at stage 42, stage 51, and
7-year-old adult. All slides contained sections that were
taken at multiple rostrocaudal levels.
Generation and characterization of a
XIF3 antiserum
XIF3 and Xenopus NF-L antisera (Berkeley Antibody,
Richmond, CA) were generated in rabbits that were im-
munized with a synthetic peptide derived from the car-
boxy end of the C-terminal tail domain of the respective
nIF
proteins
(XIF3,
QVVTESRKEQSSEGE;
NF-L,
GESKGDEAAEESE; Peptide Technologies, Gaithersburg,
MD). Peptides were coupled by glutaraldehyde to keyhole
limpet hemocyanin prior to injection.
The antisera were characterized on Western blots as
described previously (Zhao and Szaro, 1994, 1997b).
Brieﬂy stated, juvenile frog spinal cord or brain was ho-
mogenized in 2.5% sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and 8 M
urea. Total protein from spinal cord (5 mg/mm well width)
or brain (10 mg/mm well width) was separated in an SDS-
10% polyacrylamide minigel and transferred to nitrocel-
lulose membrane. Membrane strips were processed by
using peroxidase-coupled secondary antibodies (0.5 mg/ml;
Kirkegaard and Perry, Gaithersburg, MD) and diamino-
benzidine (DAB) as a chromogen. XIF3 and Xenopus NF-L
antisera were tested at different dilutions and compared
with equivalent dilutions of preimmune serum from the
same rabbit to determine speciﬁcity. In addition, several
antibodies against other IF proteins were also used for
comparison: antisera against XNIF (LC2 rabbit polyclonal
antiserum diluted 1:500; Charnas et al., 1992) and vimen-
tin (rabbit polyclonal antiserum diluted 1:500; Szaro and
Gainer, 1988b), and a monoclonal antibody to an epitope
from the 2b portion of the rod domain of IF proteins (IFA
hybridoma supernatant, 100% or 50% dilution; Pruss et
al., 1981).
Immunocytochemical procedures
Immunocytochemistry was performed with the XIF3
antiserum, its preimmune serum, and a mouse monoclo-
nal antibody to proliferating cell nuclear antigen (PCNA;
Sigma). Optimal ﬁxation conditions for using the XIF3
and PCNA antibodies in immunocytochemistry were the
same as those used for in situ hybridization. This allowed
for better comparisons of the distribution of XIF3 mRNA
with its protein and with PCNA, because immunocyto-
chemistry could be done on sections neighboring those
that were processed for in situ hybridization (stage 51
tadpole, juvenile frog spinal cord and head, and adult frog
brain).
Immunocytochemistry was performed as described by
Szaro and Gainer (1988a). Slides containing perfusion-
ﬁxed and sectioned tissues were postﬁxed in ice-cold meth-
anol. Nonspeciﬁc binding sites were blocked in 10% fetal
calf serum, and endogenous peroxidase activity was sup-
pressed with 0.15% hydrogen peroxide/12% methanol in
Tris-buffered saline, pH 7.6. Slides were incubated subse-
quently in primary antibody overnight at 4°C and then in
appropriate peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibody (2
mg/ml; Kirkegaard and Perry). After washes, slides were
developed in a peroxidase reaction solution using DAB
and nickel chloride as chromogens and a glucose oxidase
reaction as the source of peroxide (Szaro and Gainer,
1988a). Negative controls consisted of slides incubated
with XIF3 preimmune serum (for XIF3 staining) or 10%
fetal calf serum without primary antibody (for PCNA).
XIF3 antiserum and the preimmune serum were used at
dilutions ranging from 1:1,000 to 1:10,000, and the PCNA
antiserum was used at dilutions from 1:1,000 to 1:5,000.
Phylogenetic tree inference
To construct a phylogenetic tree for Xenopus nIF pro-
teins, we used the sequences of Xenopus IF proteins that
are expressed in nervous system along with those of IF
proteins from a mammal (human) and from ﬁsh (either
zebraﬁsh or goldﬁsh, as available) that had the highest
amino acid similarities with the Xenopus proteins, and
Xenopus lamin A (a ubiquitously expressed IF protein
found in the nuclear envelope). The 20 nucleotide se-
quences obtained from GenBank were as follows, with
accession
numbers
in
parentheses:
human
NF-L
(NM006158), Xenopus NF-L (M86654), zebraﬁsh geﬁltin
(U89710), Xenopus xeﬁltin (U63711), human a-internexin
(NM004692), Xenopus XNIF (M86653), goldﬁsh NF-M
(L09741), human NF-M (NM005382), Xenopus NF-M1
(U85969), Xenopus NF-M2 (U85970), zebraﬁsh plasticin
(U89709), human peripherin (NM006262), Xenopus XIF3
(X16570), zebraﬁsh vimentin (AF069994), human vimen-
tin (NM003380), Xenopus vimentin 1 (X16843), Xenopus
vimentin 4 (X16844), human nestin (X65964), Xenopus
tanabin (M99387), and Xenopus lamin A (X06345).
The amino acid sequences were inferred from the nucle-
otide sequences with the “Translate” program of the Wis-
consin Package software [version 10, UNIX; Genetics
Computer Group (GCG), Madison, WI] according to the
coding domains given in the GenBank entries. However,
before the GenBank nucleotide sequence of XIF3 could be
derived properly, it was necessary to delete a T at position
88, which is absent in the published sequence (Sharpe et
al., 1989) and whose presence in the GenBank sequence
disrupts the reading frame. All 20, full-length, amino-acid
sequences were then aligned by using PileUP in GCG.
This sequence alignment (not shown) revealed that only
the rod domains of the IF proteins were alignable among
all the sequences. Because phylogenetic trees should be
constructed by using only regions of alignable sequences,
515
XENOPUS PERIPHERIN EXPRESSION
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

the sequences were realigned by using only the rod do-
main. We also removed 42 amino acids from the sequence
of the rod domain of Xenopus lamin A, which corresponds
to an exon that is absent from all vertebrate cytoplasmic
IF proteins.
The resulting protein sequence alignment (not shown,
but available from B.G.S. on request) was used as input to
the phylogenetic tree inference program. PAUP* (version
4.0 b2a for Macintosh computers; Swofford, 1998). Phylo-
genetic trees were inferred by both parsimony and dis-
tance methods by using a PROTPARS matrix (Felsen-
stein, 1993) to weight amino-acid replacements according
to the genetic code. The optimality criterion for distance
trees was minimum evolution (ME). To search for optimal
parsimony and ME trees, we performed 20 heuristic
searches with random input order of the sequences for
both methods. To evaluate these trees for support of in-
ternal branches, we performed 10,000 bootstrap replica-
tions using heuristic algorithms for both methods. Xeno-
pus lamin A was chosen to provide an outgroup for rooting
the IF tree, because cytoplasmic IF proteins are believed
to be derived from an ancestral nuclear lamin (Dodemont
et al., 1990).
RESULTS
Early neuronal expression of XIF3 mRNA
Some of the general features of anatomical patterns of
expression have been documented previously for XIF3
(Sharpe et al., 1989; Goldstone and Sharpe, 1998) and the
type IV nIF proteins (NF-L and XNIF: Charnas et al.,
1992; NF-M: Sharpe, 1988; Szaro et al., 1989; Gervasi and
Szaro, 1997; xeﬁltin: Zhao and Szaro, 1997b; NF-H: Szaro
et al., 1989) during Xenopus development. These studies
provided neither direct comparisons of levels of XIF3 ex-
pression relative to those of the type IV nIFs during early
axonal development, nor information on XIF3 expression
in older tadpoles through adult, nor anatomic data on the
emergence of the expression of the low-molecular-mass,
type IV nIF proteins (XNIF, xeﬁltin, and NF-L) for neu-
ronal cell bodies. For comparisons of levels of expression,
in situ hybridization is much better than immunocyto-
chemistry, because cRNA probes of similar GC content
and length vary much less in their physical binding prop-
erties to their respective targets than antibodies. More-
over, speciﬁc activities can be matched more easily among
cRNA probes than among antibodies. Thus, for the ﬁrst
part of our study, we made cRNA probes of XIF3, NF-L,
xeﬁltin, and XNIF, incorporating similar amounts of
digoxigenin into probes of comparable length. Whenever
direct comparisons were intended, these probes were used
in parallel.
As determined by wholemount in situ hybridization, by
stage 18 (neural tube stage), XIF3 mRNA was already
abundant within neurons along the entire length of the
spinal cord and in trigeminal ganglia (Fig. 1A, left). We
found XIF3 labeling in Rohon Beard neurons (located in
two dense rows within the spinal cord), motoneurons, and
interneurons, and also in extramedullary sensory neu-
rons, which are scattered along the outside of the spinal
cord (Roberts and Clarke, 1982). These neurons are
among the earliest to differentiate, withdrawing from the
mitotic cycle during gastrulation (Lamborghini, 1980) and
initiating axon outgrowth between stage 20 and stage 22
(Davies et al., 1982; Jacobson and Huang, 1985; Harten-
stein, 1993). Thus, we conﬁrmed that XIF3 is expressed in
primary neurons prior to axonal outgrowth. We also ob-
served diffuse staining above background in the anterior
neuroectoderm, as noted by Goldstone and Sharpe (1998).
At these stages, none of the type IV nIF mRNAs could be
detected.
Fig. 1.
Early expression of Xenopus laevis peripherin (XIF3) de-
termined by wholemount in situ hybridization of embryos hybridized
with cRNA probes equivalently labeled with digoxigenin. A: An-
terodorsal view of stage 18 embryos hybridized with XIF3 antisense
(left) or sense (right) probe. B,C: Lateral views of stage 22 embryos
hybridized with antisense probes to XIF3 (B) or Xenopus neuronal
intermediate ﬁlament (XNIF; C). Principal structures containing la-
beled cells are indicated by labeled arrowheads. o, Olfactory pit; rh,
rhombencephalon; sc, spinal cord; tg, trigeminal ganglion. Scale
bars 5 500 mm in A and C (C also applies to B).
516
C. GERVASI ET AL.
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

By stage 22 (early tailbud), XIF3 mRNA expression had
increased, appearing in neurons throughout more anterior
nervous system and in the olfactory placode (Fig. 1B).
Olfactory sensory neurons extend axons at later stages
(stage 28; Klein and Graziadei, 1983); thus, XIF3 expres-
sion preceded initiation of axonal outgrowth in these neu-
rons as well. This is in stark contrast to the other nIF
proteins, which were undetectable at stage 18 and emerge
only after axon initiation (Szaro et al., 1989; Charnas et
al., 1992; Gervasi and Szaro, 1997; Zhao and Szaro,
1997b). The relative abundance of XIF3 compared with
XNIF, which is the earliest of the type IV low-molecular-
mass nIF proteins to emerge in development, is illustrated
at stage 22 (Fig. 1C). In contrast to XIF3 mRNA expres-
sion (Fig. 1B), that of XNIF was faint and was limited to
the rhombencephalon and to trigeminal ganglia.
Throughout early neural development, when neural cell
proliferation and early axonal outgrowth is highest, XIF3
remained the most abundant of the low-molecular-mass
nIF mRNAs. It increased steadily through stage 32 (just
prior to hatching). In tadpoles at hatching (stage 33/34–
35/36), speciﬁc XIF3 labeling (Fig. 2A) was detected
throughout the CNS (eye, brain, and spinal cord) and
within the peripheral nervous system (cranial ganglia V,
VII, IX, and X and in the olfactory placode) in the same
pattern described by Goldstone and Sharpe (1998). We
also found additional XIF3-labeled cells in the anterior
trunk along pathways that have been described previously
for migrating neural crest cells (Krotoski et al., 1988), and,
from stage 33/34 onward, we detected XIF3-labeled cells
in the heart, which most likely were cardiac ganglionic
neurons (Heathcote, 1997).
By hatching, the levels of expression of the type IV nIF
mRNAs had increased, and their patterns of expression
began to resemble those of juvenile frogs. For example,
XNIF mRNA (Fig. 2B) showed its greatest levels of ex-
pression in the spinal cord through the mesencephalon
and showed lower levels of expression in the prosenceph-
alon and the olfactory placode, a distribution comparable
to that seen in juvenile frogs (Zhao and Szaro, 1997a).
However, even in regions of the nervous system in which
XNIF mRNA was relatively abundant, such as the retina,
it still was less abundant than XIF3 mRNA. The general
patterns of expression for the other two type IV low-
molecular-mass nIF mRNAs, xeﬁltin and NF-L, also re-
sembled those seen in juvenile frogs (Zhao and Szaro,
Fig. 2.
Wholemount in situ hybridization with equivalently la-
beled probes for the four low-molecular-mass neuronal intermediate
ﬁlament (nIF) mRNAs in newly hatched (stage 33/34–35/36) tadpoles.
Embryos were hybridized with cRNA probes to XIF3 (A), XNIF (B),
xeﬁltin (C), and low-molecular-weight neuroﬁlament protein (NF-L;
D) under parallel conditions to provide an indication of the relative
abundance of each. Specimens were photographed without clearing to
avoid confusion from underlying structures. The right-left axis of D
was reversed to match the orientation of the other photomicrographs.
m, Mesencephalon; o, olfactory pit; p, prosencephalon; rh, rhomben-
cephalon; sc, spinal cord. Roman numerals indicate respective cranial
ganglia. Scale bar 5 300 mm.
517
XENOPUS PERIPHERIN EXPRESSION
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

1997a). Xeﬁltin ﬁrst appeared in rhombencephalon at
stage 28, several hours after XNIF emerged there (stages
22–24). By stage 33/34 (Fig. 2C), xeﬁltin was more abun-
dant than XNIF in the forebrain and retina but was less
abundant than XNIF in the cranial ganglia, hindbrain,
and spinal cord. The level of xeﬁltin mRNA was greater
than that of XIF3 only in the olfactory placode. Xenopus
NF-L, the last of the low-molecular-mass nIF proteins to
emerge during development (Charnas et al., 1992), was
also the least abundant and the most restricted of the four
nIF proteins examined at hatching (Fig. 2D). Even so, its
expression at stage 33/34 resembled the general pattern
seen in juvenile frogs (Zhao and Szaro, 1997a). It was most
abundant in the hindbrain and spinal cord and was re-
stricted to only a few isolated patches within the prosen-
cephalon and mesencephalon, which corresponded to the
NF-L expression seen in juvenile frogs (olfactory areas,
hypothalamic and motor tegmental neurons, and scat-
tered neurons within the optic tecta; Zhao and Szaro,
1997a). NF-L also appeared in the trigeminal ganglia but
was not expressed yet in ganglia VII–X, which are
younger, or in the retina, although it is found within these
structures by the time of metamorphosis.
In summary, during early neural development, the ex-
pression of NF-L, xeﬁltin, and XNIF began only after the
initiation of axonal outgrowth, and it continued to in-
crease throughout development. By hatching, although
the proteins were less abundant, the general patterns of
expression of NF-L, XNIF, and xeﬁltin already resembled
the patterns of expression seen in juvenile frogs (Zhao and
Szaro, 1997a). In contrast, during early neural develop-
ment (from neurula through hatching), when cellular pro-
liferation and axonal outgrowth are most vigorous, XIF3
remained the most widely distributed and abundantly
expressed low-molecular-mass nIF protein.
Decline of early neuronal expression of
XIF3 after hatching
In the spinal cord, XIF3 mRNA expression in neurons
gradually diminished after hatching, whereas expression
of the type IV nIF mRNAs continued to increase. This is
illustrated in Figure 3, which compares the anatomic dis-
tributions and relative abundance of XIF3 (Fig. 3A,C,E)
and XNIF (Fig. 3B,D,F) mRNAs in late tailbud embryos
(stages 28–32) with those at stage 42. In wholemounts of
late tailbud embryos (shown in a lateral view in Fig. 3A),
XIF3 labeling was intense throughout the spinal cord,
whereas XNIF labeling (Fig. 3B) was more restricted. In
sections at these stages, the most intense levels of XIF3
staining were seen in Rohon Beard neurons (Fig. 3C,
arrowheads; Roberts and Clarke, 1982). Less intense XIF3
labeling was also seen distributed diffusely throughout
more central and ventral regions of transverse sections of
spinal cord (Fig. 3C) in a manner similar to that seen in
more anterior, undifferentiated neuroectoderm at earlier
stages (Goldstone and Sharpe, 1998). In contrast, XNIF
expression at these stages was restricted to neurons (Fig.
3D, arrowheads), as identiﬁed by morphologic criteria
(Roberts and Clarke, 1982).
By swimming tadpole stages (Fig. 3E), XIF3 mRNA had
disappeared from most differentiated spinal neurons but
still was found in nonneuronal cells of the central ﬁeld,
which surround the central canal and include undifferen-
tiated neuroepithelial cells (Roberts and Clarke, 1982). At
stage 42, XIF3 was also abundant in neurons of the dorsal
root ganglia (Fig. 3E) and the cranial ganglia. By compar-
ison, the distribution of XNIF mRNA (Fig. 3F) comple-
mented that of XIF3 at stage 42: XNIF expression was
high in spinal neurons but was absent from cells of the
central ﬁeld.
XIF3 mRNA expression in peripheral
neurons through adulthood
Although XIF3 expression declined in most CNS neu-
rons, it remained abundant in mature peripheral neurons
throughout life, like the expression of peripherin in adult
mammals (Leonard et al., 1988; Parysek et al., 1988) and
of plasticin in a few subpopulations of peripheral neurons
of zebraﬁsh (Asch et al., 1998; Canger et al., 1998). For
example, XIF3 mRNA was abundant in neurons of the
dorsal root ganglia (illustrated for juvenile frog in Fig. 4A)
as well as in all cranial ganglia and some associated motor
nuclei within the brain (not shown), in motoneurons of the
spinal cord ventral horn (juvenile frog; Fig. 4C), in pregan-
glionic sympathetic neurons of the lateral gray matter in
the juvenile frog spinal cord (as identiﬁed by their posi-
tion; see, e.g., Fig. 7A), and in enteric neurons of the gut
(illustrated for a stage 51 tadpole in Fig. 4E). In addition
to these cells with peripherally projecting axons, XIF3
mRNA was also found in a few isolated CNS neurons,
including reticular neurons of the hindbrain (see, e.g., Fig.
8A,C) and subsets of tegmental neurons (not illustrated).
A similar situation occurs for mammalian peripherin,
which is found in clusters of phylogenetically older neu-
rons scattered throughout the brain, including reticular
and tegmental neurons (Portier et al., 1984; Leonard et
al., 1988; Parysek and Goldman, 1988).
Although XIF3 expression became restricted to a few
populations of neurons, the other nIF proteins became
widely expressed among neurons (illustrated for XNIF in
Fig. 4B,D; other examples shown in Figs. 7B, 8B,D; see
also Zhao and Szaro, 1997a). Even in neurons in which
XIF3 continued to be expressed through adulthood, the
particular type IV nIF mRNAs that were present in those
cells were usually more abundant.
XIF3 mRNA expression in regions of the
CNS containing proliferating cells
The CNS of anamniotes retains a high degree of plas-
ticity into adulthood, with continued axonal and dendritic
remodeling and cell proliferation throughout life. For ex-
ample, in both ﬁsh and frog, the retinal ciliary margin
generates new neurons and glia throughout life (Johns
and Easter, 1977; Straznicky and Hiscock, 1984). In the
goldﬁsh retina, this phenomenon is reﬂected in the nIF
composition of retinal cells, in which the type III nIF,
plasticin, is expressed preferentially in the youngest
RGCs, adjacent to the ciliary margin (Fuchs et al., 1994).
In stage 51 Xenopus tadpoles, as in ﬁsh, XIF3 expression
was restricted to those RGCs closest to the ciliary margin;
however, unlike in ﬁsh, it was present within the prolif-
erating ciliary margin as well (Fig. 5A,C). In the inner
nuclear layer, XIF3 labeling was faint and diffuse, extend-
ing throughout the retina. The type IV nIF mRNAs were
found in RGCs throughout the retina but not in the ciliary
margin (illustrated for XNIF in Fig. 5B,D).
XIF3 mRNA was also found in other regions of the
nervous system in which neuronal proliferation is known
to be high. For example, in vertebrates, neuronal precur-
sors for olfactory epithelial sensory neurons reside in its
518
C. GERVASI ET AL.
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

basal layer (Calof et al., 1998). Basal layer cells expressed
XIF3 mRNA (illustrated for stage 51 tadpole in Fig. 6A),
whereas mature neurons elsewhere within the olfactory
epithelium expressed xeﬁltin instead (Fig. 6B). Other pro-
liferative zones of the stage 51 tadpole CNS surround the
ventricles of the brain and central canal of the spinal cord
(Thors et al., 1982). XIF3 staining in these regions was
lightly visible in young embryos and tadpoles (see Fig.
3C,E), but it became exceptionally pronounced by stage 51
(Fig. 9C) and persisted after metamorphosis (Fig. 7A).
These cells expressed none of the other low-molecular-
mass nIF mRNAs (illustrated for XNIF in Fig. 7B).

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

XIF3 mRNA expression remained high in ventricular
regions of the brain, even in 7-year-old adults. Cross sec-
tions of brain from the 7-year-old frog (Fig. 8C,D) were vis-
ibly much larger than those of the juvenile (Fig. 8A,B). None-
theless, the overall pattern of XIF3 expression (Fig. 8A,C)
was the same in the older brain and the juvenile brain,
including the ventricular areas, although the intensity of
staining was slightly less in the adult. Similar to the younger
tadpoles, cells in ventricular areas of postmetamorphic frogs
lacked detectable expression of any of the other nIF proteins,
although these proteins were expressed widely by mature
neurons (illustrated for XNIF in Fig. 8B,D).
Because the expression of type III nIF proteins has not
been reported in proliferative zones of ﬁsh or of mammals,
Fig. 4.

C. GERVASI ET AL.
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

we wanted to reduce the possibility that our probe was
hybridizing to message from nonneuronal IF proteins,
such as vimentin. Thus, we also used a second probe to
XIF3 that targeted only the 826 nucleotides that span the
highly divergent C-terminal tail and 39-untranslated re-
gions of XIF3 mRNA, thereby avoiding the more conserved
regions of IF proteins. When it was tested on sections of
stage 51 tadpole, juvenile, and adult CNS, this second probe
yielded exactly the same staining patterns that were seen
with the full-length cRNA probe (not illustrated).
To assay whether XIF3-positive cells in ventricular and
subventricular areas of the brain and spinal cord were
truly proliferating, we stained sections of stage 51 tadpole,
juvenile, and 7-year-old adult CNS with the PCNA anti-
body. This antibody recognizes a highly conserved epitope
of PCNA (Waseem and Lane, 1990), which is an auxiliary
protein of DNA polymerase d and a marker for cells in
S-phase in Xenopus embryos (Leibovici et al., 1992).
Throughout stage 51 tadpole, cells were labeled by the
PCNA antibody in regions known to contain proliferating
cells but were unlabeled in regions containing postmitotic
cells. Within the nervous system, all cell groups stained by
the PCNA antibody in proliferative zones also expressed
XIF3 mRNA. For example, Figure 9B shows PCNA stain-
ing in the retinal ciliary margin, which was shown previ-
ously to express XIF3 (Fig. 5C). PCNA staining also oc-
curred in the dorsal central ﬁeld of the spinal cord (Fig.
9A). This group of cells also expressed XIF3 mRNA. Al-
though all groups of cells that stained for PCNA were
found amid populations of cells that expressed XIF3, some
populations of cells that expressed XIF3 lacked PCNA.
The latter included cells of the ventral central ﬁeld and
ventral motor neurons.
Consistent with declining rates of proliferation after
metamorphosis, PCNA staining in ventricular and sub-
ventricular areas of the CNS decreased considerably in
juvenile frogs (Fig. 9D). After metamorphosis, PCNA
staining was visible only at higher magniﬁcation in iso-
lated cells surrounding the ventricles and central canal
(not visible at the magniﬁcation shown in Fig. 9). Never-
theless, cells in these regions continued to express XIF3
mRNA abundantly (see Fig. 7A). These results raised the
possibility that XIF3 mRNA is expressed either in re-
cently postmitotic, premigratory neurons or in some other
nonneuronal cells in these regions.

Expression of XIF3 protein in radial glia
and subsets of neurons
An antibody was raised to a synthetic peptide from the
highly divergent C-terminal tail domain of XIF3, both to
help us classify the ventricular and subventricular cells
that express XIF3 mRNA and to conﬁrm that they express
XIF3 protein. We ﬁrst tested the speciﬁcity of this anti-
body on Western blots. The XIF3 antiserum recognized an
intense band of 56 kDa (Fig. 10a,b, arrowhead) and a
second, much fainter band immediately below it, whereas
the preimmune serum stained nothing (Fig. 10a). The
56-kDa band comigrated with one of the bands stained by
the IFA antibody (Fig. 10b), which recognizes an epitope
conserved among IF proteins (Pruss et al., 1981). We com-
pared the staining obtained with the XIF3 antiserum with
that obtained with antisera against two other nIF pro-
teins, NF-L and XNIF, and with that obtained from an
antiserum against Xenopus vimentin. We were especially
interested in comparing the XIF3 and vimentin antisera on
Western blots, because the protein distribution observed
with the XIF3 antiserum, as described below, overlapped
extensively with that of vimentin (Szaro and Gainer, 1988b;
Szaro et al., 1989; Charnas et al., 1992). Neither the intense
56-kDa band nor the faint secondary band comigrated with
the bands that were stained by the antibodies against NF-L,
XNIF (Fig. 10a), or vimentin (Fig. 10b). It is also highly
unlikely that the XIF3 antiserum is able to cross react with
xeﬁltin, NF-M, or NF-H, because their apparent molecular
weights on Western blots (xeﬁltin: 65 kDa; Zhao and Szaro,
1997b; NF-M: 175 kDa; NF-H: 205 kDa; Szaro and Gainer,
1988a) are quite different and because the sequence of the
synthetic peptide used to make the XIF3 antiserum is not
found anywhere in the sequences of xeﬁltin and NF-M (nor,
for that matter, in any other known Xenopus IF protein).
Similarly, the anatomic patterns of expression of
cytokeratin- and GFAP-like proteins in Xenopus are also
quite different (Szaro and Gainer, 1988b) from the pattern
obtained with the XIF3 antiserum. Thus, we did not evalu-
ate directly any antibodies against these other ﬁve Xenopus
IF proteins in this study.

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

To map the anatomic distribution of XIF3 protein, im-
munocytochemical experiments were performed on sec-
tions of stage 51 tadpole, juvenile, and 7-year-old adult
frog CNS. To facilitate comparisons, the sections were
neighbors of those that were used previously for XIF3 in
situ hybridization studies. Neuronal staining with XIF3
antiserum occurred in subsets of axons within the periph-
eral nerve (Fig. 11B, dorsal root; Fig. 11F, cranial nerve
segment) and within cell bodies and proximal axonal seg-
ments in dorsal root ganglia (Fig. 11D,G) and cranial
ganglia (not shown). This ﬁnding conﬁrmed the classiﬁca-
tion of XIF3 as a neuronal IF protein. In addition to
labeling axons, the XIF3 antiserum clearly stained radial
glial processes and occasionally stained their cell bodies
(shown from multiple rostrocaudal levels of juvenile frog
spinal cord in Fig. 11A–E). The cell bodies of these glia are
located within the same ventricular and subventricular
regions of the brain and spinal cord that were labeled
intensely and uniformly by the XIF3 cRNA probe (see Fig.
7A). Radially oriented processes extend from these cell
bodies to end feet at the pial surface. The location of their
cell bodies and the characteristic appearance of their
Golgi-like staining enabled us to identify unambiguously
these XIF3-positive cells as radial glia (King, 1966). In-
tense staining was also observed in retinal Mu¨eller cells
(not shown), which have cell bodies in the inner nuclear
layer, where XIF3 mRNA was detected diffusely. Thus, in
Xenopus, XIF3 is a glial IF protein as well as a neuronal IF
protein.
Phylogenetic relationship of XIF3 to other
known nIF proteins
To provide an evolutionary framework for interpreting
patterns of XIF3 expression, we constructed phylogenetic
trees of Xenopus IF proteins found in the nervous system
and included probable orthologs of each from representa-
tive mammals and ﬁshes. These trees tested the hypoth-
esis that XIF3, peripherin, and plasticin are orthologous
type III nIF proteins. If these three proteins are orthologs,
then the prediction is that trees constructed from these
sequences should mirror the known tree for the species
(i.e., the frog and mammal sequences should be related
Fig. 8.

regions (vr) near the obex (ob) and in reticular neurons (rf). B,D: The same regions are also identiﬁed in
the sections that were stained for XNIF. A–D are shown at the same magniﬁcation. Scale bar 5 300 mm.

 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

more closely to each other than either is to the ﬁsh se-
quence).
Figure 12 presents the ME distance tree (score 5
4.61325) inferred from the alignment of the rod domains of
these proteins. The parsimony tree (length 5 1,796 re-
placement events; not shown) was identical to the ME
tree, except that the clade containing the NF-Ms was
moved 1 node deeper in the tree, so that it appeared as a
sister clade to the type III and remaining type IV IF
proteins. We present only the ME tree, because it is more
consistent with the known distributions of introns within
type III and type IV IF genes (Steinert and Roop, 1988).
Moreover, for the purposes of determining whether XIF3,
plasticin, and peripherin are orthologs, the implications of
the ME and parsimony trees we obtained were identical.
The bootstrap values associated with most of the inter-
nal branches on this tree showed strong statistical support
(i.e., .75%; Hillis and Bull, 1993) for the depicted associ-
ations. Most importantly for this study, ﬁsh plasticin,
mammalian peripherin, and frog XIF3 clearly formed
their own clade with 100% bootstrap support. Likewise,
the bootstrap value for associating XIF3 more closely with
peripherin than with plasticin also received strong sup-
port (76%). Conversely, the tree that grouped XIF3 more
closely with plasticin received only weak support (23%).
Any other possible association between XIF3 and the
other IF proteins received virtually no support in the
bootstrap analyses, with the sum of all other possible
associations accounting for ,1% of the bootstrap trees.
This can be taken to mean that placement of XIF3 outside
of the XIF3/plasticin/peripherin clade is ruled out statis-
tically by the bootstrap method (Felsenstein, 1988). These
results support the hypothesis that these three nIF pro-
teins are indeed orthologs, because their gene tree mir-
rored the known species tree. The same situation was seen
among the vimentins, which are also considered to be

C. GERVASI ET AL.
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. orthologs (Herrmann et al., 1989). Moreover, the vimentin
clade and the XIF3 clade grouped together with 100%
bootstrap support, which is consistent with their classiﬁ-
cation as type III IF proteins. The relationships among the
other nIF proteins were more complex, and, because they
are less relevant to our study of XIF3 expression, they are
discussed below.
DISCUSSION
Our expression studies extended earlier work that had
mapped some of the general features of nIF protein ex-
pression in Xenopus (Szaro et al., 1989; Charnas et al.,
1992; Zhao and Szaro, 1997a,b; Goldstone and Sharpe,
1998). We showed that, from the earliest stages of axonal
outgrowth through hatching, XIF3 was the most abun-
dantly and widely expressed of the low-molecular-mass
nIFs in Xenopus. In contrast, XNIF, xeﬁltin, and NF-L
ﬁrst emerged in the same regions in which they are ex-
pressed in adults, in a pattern that was established grad-
ually by the time of hatching. After metamorphosis, XIF3
expression in neurons became restricted to those with
peripherally projecting axons and to a few remnant pop-
ulations of phylogenetically old CNS neurons. Unexpect-
edly, XIF3 mRNA was also expressed widely in ventricu-
lar regions of the brain and in the central ﬁeld of the
spinal cord through adulthood. These regions have few if
any fully differentiated neurons, but they overlap exten-
sively with populations of proliferating cells (as evidenced
by staining with an antibody to PCNA) as well as radial
glia (as evidenced by staining with a new antiserum to
XIF3). Thus, in Xenopus, XIF3 is both a neuronal IF
protein and a glial IF protein.
In addition to these expression studies, we also built
phylogenetic trees to determine the relationships among
various IF proteins found in the vertebrate nervous sys-
tem. These trees strongly supported the hypothesis that
XIF3, plasticin, and peripherin are orthologs. Thus, vari-
ations in the expression of these three type III nIF pro-
teins can be thought of as regulatory changes that have
occurred at the “same” genetic locus along each lineage. In
the rest of this discussion, ﬁrst, we address the implica-
tions of the phylogenetic trees for relationships among and
the nomenclature of various vertebrate IF proteins; then,
these conclusions are extended to interpret the observed
phylogenetic differences in the expressions of type III nIF
proteins during vertebrate neural development.
Phylogenetic relationships among Xenopus
nIF proteins
To construct a phylogenetic framework for interpreting
variations in the expression of the type III nIF proteins in
different vertebrate groups, we inferred phylogenetic trees
by parsimony and by distance. The simplest hypothesis
consistent with these trees is that mammalian peripherin,
ﬁsh plasticin, and Xenopus XIF3 are orthologs. This con-
clusion is based on the following observations: First, XIF3,
peripherin, and plasticin branch together as a clade in the

antibody (IFA) directed against an epitope common to all intermedi-
ate ﬁlament proteins. Labeled bars on the left of each group of blots
indicate the positions of prestained protein molecular mass standards
(molecular masses in kDa). Arrowheads on the right of each strip
point to the bands that were stained by each respective antibody,
except for the strip labeled IFA, in which the arrowhead points only to
the band that comigrated with XIF3.

C. GERVASI ET AL.
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

phylogenetic trees. Second, within this clade, the branch-
ing order of XIF3, peripherin, and plasticin mirrors the
known phylogenetic relationship among frogs, mammals,
and ﬁshes, respectively. These relationships have high
bootstrap support (Fig. 12). Additional (but less direct)
support for this conclusion is that, in these trees, the
relative
branch
lengths
within
the
XIF3/peripherin/
plasticin clade are comparable to those within the vimen-
tin and NF-M clades, which are also considered to be
orthologs.
The alignments used to build these phylogenetic trees
were restricted to the rod domain of IF proteins, because
phylogenetic trees should be constructed from regions of
mutually alignable sequence, and only the rod domain could
be aligned among all of the IF proteins in the analysis.
Sequences from the head and C-terminal tail domains of
XIF3, plasticin, and peripherin, nonetheless, could be
aligned with each other as a group, separate from the other
IF proteins. This ﬁnding further supports the grouping of
XIF3, peripherin, and plasticin into a separate clade. More-
over, the head domains of peripherin (99 amino acids long)
and XIF3 (90 amino acids long) are more similar to each
other in length than either is to that of plasticin (75 amino
acids), and the C-terminal tail domains of peripherin and
XIF3 share considerably more sequence identity with each
other (72%) than either does with that of plasticin (44% and
57%, respectively). These similarities further mirror the spe-
cies tree, which places frogs and mammals more closely to
one another than to ﬁshes and, thus, are consistent with the
orthology of peripherin, plasticin, and XIF3. Indeed, these
were the observations that originally led others to suggest
that these three proteins are probably orthologs (Sharpe et
al., 1989; Canger et al., 1998). Our phylogenetic trees, which
take into account the evolutionary changes needed to explain
the alignment of the rod domains, greatly strengthen this
hypothesis. Additional testing of the orthology of these pro-
teins awaits the sequencing of the genomes of these organ-
isms, which will reveal the synteny of surrounding genetic
loci and may reveal additional members of the IF superfam-
ily.
The orthology of XIF3, plasticin, and peripherin further
suggests that these three nIF proteins should more prop-
erly be referred to as Xenopus, ﬁsh, and mammal periph-
erin, respectively, following the convention that the name
of the ﬁrst protein described in the literature (peripherin;
Portier et al., 1984) should be given to the genetic locus.
Thus, we shall adopt this nomenclature for the remainder
of this Discussion. It should be kept in mind, however,
that genetic orthology means only that proteins are de-
rived from the “same” genetic locus at the time of species
divergence and does not necessarily imply that proteins
are functionally identical, especially if the time of diver-
gence has been sufﬁcient to allow for signiﬁcant structural
changes. With respect to the peripherins, for example,
variations in the lengths and sequences of head and
C-terminal tail domains conceivably may have resulted in
different assembly properties or in changes in cytoskeletal
organization. Thus, potential structural changes will have
to be considered along with variations in expression pat-
terns before a full understanding can be reached of the
role played by peripherins in early axonal outgrowth
among various vertebrates.
Our phylogenetic trees helped to clarify relationships
among some of the other nIF proteins as well. For example,
the trees fully supported the previous identiﬁcations of NF-
Ls, vimentins, and NF-Ms as orthologs but did not support
reclassifying any of the remaining ﬁsh or frog type IV nIF
proteins as a-internexin orthologs. For example, XNIF was
originally given a unique name (Charnas et al., 1992), be-
cause it shared less sequence identity with its closest mam-
malian relative, a-internexin, than Xenopus and mamma-
lian NF-Ls shared with each other. The relative placements
of XNIF and a-internexin within the phylogenetic trees sup-
ports this nomenclature and further suggests that the gene
duplication that gave rise to XNIF occurred early in verte-
Fig. 12.
Minimum evolution (ME) phylogenetic tree of sequenced
IF proteins found in the Xenopus laevis nervous system and their
closest known counterparts in ﬁsh and mammal based on alignments
of amino acids of the rod domain. Numbers to the left of the branch
points indicate the percent of 10,000 bootstrap replicates that support
that branch. The tree shows branch lengths as proportional to evolu-
tionary distance (mean substitutions per residue; scale indicated be-
low the tree). Branch points labeled with solid circles represent those
that logically must have duplications to be able to account for both the
tree topology and the presence of the Xenopus IF proteins. The branch
point labeled with an open circle indicates a duplication that is not
logically required by the above criterion but is likely due to the length
of the branches and the very different expression patterns of human
nestin and Xenopus tanabin. Unlabeled branch points represent spe-
ciation events. NF-M, medium-molecular-mass neuroﬁlament pro-
tein.

XENOPUS PERIPHERIN EXPRESSION
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

brate evolution. Thus, XNIF orthologs either do exist or did
exist in other vertebrate groups. Also, xeﬁltin and geﬁltin
were originally considered probable orthologs, but their re-
lationship to a-internexin was less clear (Zhao and Szaro,
1997a; Asch et al., 1998). The phylogenetic trees were con-
sistent with the orthology of geﬁltin and xeﬁltin (thus, xeﬁl-
tin also could be referred to as Xenopus geﬁltin), and they
further suggested that ﬁsh and frog geﬁltins are paralogs of
mammalian a-internexin. The absence of one or more rep-
resentatives of XNIF, a-internexin, and geﬁltin from each
lineage suggests either that additional type IV nIF genes
await discovery or that some nIF proteins were lost along
each lineage either by gene silencing or by loss from the
genome.
The two remaining IF proteins in the phylogenetic tree,
nestin and tanabin, are also considered to be distinct
genetically. This is because tanabin expression is re-
stricted to a few populations of early neurons (Hemmati-
Brivanlou et al., 1992), whereas nestin is found in undif-
ferentiated neuroepithelia and radial glia (Lendahl et al.,
1990). The relatively long branch lengths separating hu-
man nestin from Xenopus tanabin in the tree are consis-
tent with their being either paralogs or extremely rapidly
evolving orthologs (which seems unlikely). If they are
paralogs, then additional nestin and tanabin orthologs
may exist in frogs and mammals, respectively.
Neuronal expression patterns of vertebrate
peripherins
Except for the retina, all of the neurons within the
mature Xenopus nervous system that express Xenopus
peripherin
(ventral
motoneurons,
enteric
neurons,
preganglionic sympathetic neurons, reticular neurons,
tegmental neurons, motor neurons with axons in cranial
nerves, and cranial and dorsal root ganglionic neurons)
are homologous to those that express peripherin in mam-
mals (for review, see Greene, 1989). The situation is sim-
ilar in zebraﬁsh, except that peripherin expression in ma-
ture ﬁsh appears to be more restricted than in adult frogs
or mammals (Canger et al., 1998). One of the principal
differences in peripherin expression between mammals
and anamniotes (for summary, see Table 1) occurs in
RGCs, which cannot regenerate axons in mammals but do
so readily in both ﬁsh and frog. In ﬁsh and frog retina, new
RGCs are added continually throughout life, but in mam-
mal, all RGCs differentiate at approximately the same
time in the fetus. Only young RGCs (see Table 1, imma-
ture retinal ganglion cells), which lie near the ciliary mar-
gin, express peripherins in adult goldﬁsh (Fuchs et al.,
1994) and Xenopus, whereas mammalian RGCs never ap-
pear to express peripherin, even during development (for
review, see Greene, 1989). Thus, it is tempting to specu-
late that the loss of peripherin from mammalian RGCs is
related to differences between mammals and anamniotes
in retinal development and the capacity of RGCs for ax-
onal regeneration.
The absence of peripherin in young neurons of mamma-
lian embryos (see Table 1, new neurites) is another example
that underscores the association between peripherin expres-
sion, axonal outgrowth, and the capacity for regeneration.
For example, the expression of peripherin in Xenopus and
zebraﬁsh (Canger et al., 1998) generally precedes and is
more widespread than that of the type IV nIF proteins (al-
though, in developing Xenopus, peripherin appears to be
more widespread than in zebraﬁsh). In contrast, in rat and
mouse embryos, the expression of peripherin begins concur-
rently with that of NF-L and generally is restricted to the
same populations of neurons that express peripherin in the
adult (Escurat et al., 1990; Gorham et al., 1990; Troy et al.,
1990a). The one exception to this rule during mammalian
development occurs in the largest sensory neurons, in which
peripherin, although it is initially coexpressed with NF-L,
gradually disappears as NF-L expression increases.
The association between peripherin expression and the
capacity to grow an axon appears to be especially strong
when peripherin expression is examined in regenerating
axons of ﬁsh and mammals. In mammalian sensory neu-
rons, the expression of peripherin rises during early
phases of peripheral nerve regeneration, whereas that of
NF-L diminishes (Troy et al., 1990b; Wong and Oblinger,
1990). The response of peripherin and type IV nIF protein
expression to axotomy in mammalian peripheral nerve
thus resembles that of regenerating optic axons of ze-
braﬁsh and goldﬁsh (Glasgow et al., 1992, 1994; Asch et

al., 1998). Although it still remains to be seen whether
Xenopus peripherin expression also increases during early
phases of optic nerve regeneration, the overall picture that
has now emerged in all three groups of vertebrates is that
an axonal cytoskeleton with more type III and less type IV
nIF proteins is favored in many newly forming axons
(Wong and Oblinger, 1990; Glasgow et al., 1994).
Peripherin expression in Xenopus
neuroepithelium and radial glia
The nonneuronal expression of a peripherin in prolifer-
ative zones and radial glia is unique to Xenopus, because
expression of peripherin in ﬁsh and mammal clearly is
restricted to postmitotic neurons. In previous studies, the
most abundant IF protein of Xenopus radial glia appeared
to be vimentin, with subclasses of radial glia also express-
ing cytokeratins and GFAP-like proteins (Szaro and
Gainer, 1989b). Our study now shows that a peripherin is
an additional prominent IF component of radial glia in
Xenopus. Clearly, a study similar to that presented here
for nIF proteins needs to be done to assess the relative
levels of expression of the various glial IF proteins in
Xenopus during development.
Because Xenopus peripherin is the ﬁrst peripherin or-
tholog reported in nonneuronal cells, we were particularly
concerned that our antibodies and probes may have cross
reacted with vimentin, which, in Xenopus, is the IF pro-
tein whose expression in nonneuronal cells resembles
most closely that of Xenopus peripherin (Szaro and
Gainer, 1989b). Although Xenopus glial cells also have
cytokeratins and GFAP-like proteins, these IF proteins
have distributions that are very different from that of
Xenopus peripherin (Szaro and Gainer, 1989b). The fol-
lowing observations led us to conclude that the expression
of Xenopus peripherin in nonneuronal cells was real: Two
separate cRNA probes, one a full-length probe and the
other a shorter probe targeted only to the least conserved
portion of Xenopus peripherin mRNA, yielded identical
staining patterns. The immunocytochemical results gen-
erally agreed with those of the in situ hybridization stud-
ies. Western blot analysis showed that the Xenopus pe-
ripherin antiserum did not cross react with vimentin.
Xenopus peripherin was found in some populations of
radial glia that do not express vimentin (e.g., retinal Mu¨el-
ler cells; Szaro and Gainer, 1988b), and the expression of
Xenopus peripherin has been observed previously by oth-
ers at very early stages of neural development in anterior,
undifferentiated neuroepithelium (Goldstone and Sharpe,
1998). The expression we observed in ventricular and sub-
ventricular regions at later stages, thus, appears to be an
extension of this earlier nonneuronal expression.
There is a possibility that multiple forms of Xenopus pe-
ripherin exist and that the novel expression of peripherin in
glia that we observed arose in only one of them. For example,
the ancestrally duplicated genome of Xenopus has two copies
of all type IV nIF genes cloned so far (NF-M: Gervasi and
Szaro, 1997; XNIF, NF-L, and xeﬁltin: unpublished results).
In the case of NF-M, which is the one case that we have
examined carefully (Gervasi and Szaro, 1997), the two genes
are so closely related that their respective mRNAs could only
be distinguished by using oligonucleotide probes targeted to
a limited region of the sequence, and the expression patterns
of these two forms largely overlapped. Therefore, assuming
that two peripherins exist in Xenopus, it seems unlikely that
their expression patterns would be so different that one
would be neuronal and the other would be glial. Multiple
forms of IF proteins can also be produced by alternative
splicing of transcripts. For example, zebraﬁsh peripherin
has two alternatively spliced forms (Asch et al., 1998), and
mouse peripherin has three (Landon et al., 1989). Differen-
tial expression of the various alternatively spliced forms has
not been observed in these animals, however. If alternative
splicing could account for the broader expression patterns
seen in Xenopus relative to ﬁsh and mammal, it would have
interesting evolutionary implications for the idea that type
III nIFs have retained features of an ancient class of IFs (see
below), because differential expression of alternatively
spliced IF transcripts is common among invertebrates (Way
et al., 1992; Dodemont et al., 1994). Determining the situa-
tion in Xenopus would require isolating clones from each
multiply spliced form; so far, however, only one form of
Xenopus peripherin has been found (Sharpe et al., 1989).
During development, the molecular composition of IFs
changes in glia, as it does in neurons (Tapscott et al., 1981).
In mammals, for example, nestin is expressed in uncommit-
ted neuroepithelium (Lendahl et al., 1990). As development
proceeds, it is supplemented with vimentin, which persists
in radial glia, the precursors to astrocytes and oligodendro-
cytes (Cochard and Paulin, 1984). Vimentin is replaced later
by GFAP in fully differentiated astrocytes (Bovolenta et al.,
1987). Presumably, in glia, as in neurons, these changes in
IF composition help differentiating cells meet the new struc-
tural demands that arise from changes in morphology. This
simple progression in mammals, although correct in its gen-
eral features, is actually more complex. For example, sub-
populations of both radial glia and astrocytes in mammals
can coexpress vimentin and GFAP (Levitt et al., 1983), and
some mammalian ependymal glia express cytokeratins (Vie-
hweg and Naumann, 1996). Moreover, recognition that
many IF-associated proteins, such as plectin, are in fact
distantly related members of the IF superfamily has further
increased the number of IF proteins found among mamma-
lian glia (Errante et al., 1994). In amphibians, complex pat-
terns of IF gene expression among subpopulations of glia
have been recognized for some time and have been corre-
lated there with varying capacities for plasticity and regen-
eration among regions of the nervous system (for review, see
Naujoks-Manteuffel and Roth, 1995). Nonetheless, in both
mammals and frogs, the functional implications of multiple
IF proteins in glia remain unclear. For example, these vari-
ations in IF composition may satisfy different structural
requirements among glial subtypes, or they may simply rep-
resent vestigial consequences of the emergence of differen-
tial gene expression among various cellular phenotypes dur-
ing evolution.
Because glial expression of peripherin was found only in
Xenopus, the most parsimonious explanation for how this
occurred during evolution is that this feature was ac-
quired after the Xenopus lineage diverged from the lin-
eage leading to mammals. Perhaps, then, the expression
of a peripherin in Xenopus radial glia is an adaptation
that provides added structural support over that provided
by vimentin, which may be useful, because Xenopus radial
glia persist throughout life. Given that support for this
hypothesis is based on data from just three classes of
vertebrates, other possibilities cannot be ruled out.
One such possibility is raised by comparing the positions
of vimentins and peripherins within the phylogenetic trees
(Fig. 12) to the expression patterns of these proteins (sum-
marized in Table 1). The expression pattern of Xenopus
529
XENOPUS PERIPHERIN EXPRESSION
 10969861, 2000, 3, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/1096-9861(20000731)423:3<512::AID-CNE13>3.0.CO;2-1 by Ulsan National Institute Of Science And Technology (Unist), Wiley Online Library on [28/10/2022]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

peripherin shares features not only with peripherins of ﬁsh
and mammal (e.g., expression in peripheral axons) but also
with vimentins (e.g., expression in radial glia), and periph-
erins and vimentins share a common type III ancestor. Thus,
it seems likely that the type III IF gene that was ancestral to
peripherins and vimentins may have been expressed in both
glia and neurons. This possibility seems reasonable, because
invertebrate IF genes often have much broader tissue spec-
iﬁcities than is typical for vertebrate IF genes (Dodemont et
al., 1994). When this ancestral IF gene duplicated to produce
the two daughter genes that eventually gave rise to the
vimentins and peripherins, each daughter gene at ﬁrst
would have retained features of the original ancestral IF
gene expression pattern. These features would then have
been modiﬁed along the separate lineages leading to the
modern IF proteins. Thus, the glial and neuronal expres-
sions of peripherin in Xenopus may have been derived from
this ancestral IF gene, and glial expression may then have
been lost independently along the lineages leading to cyp-
rinid ﬁshes and to mammals. Distinguishing between these
two hypotheses will require establishing whether additional
peripherin orthologs exist in vertebrate lineages bracketing
the teleost lineage and then determining their expression
patterns.
Circumstantial support for this latter hypothesis is pro-
vided by the expression patterns of the peripherins. The
ability of vertebrate CNS neurons to regenerate successfully
has been associated with the retention of so-called “primi-
tive” characteristics, including a continued capacity for neu-
ronal proliferation (Beazley et al., 1998). Such “primitive”
characteristics are often characteristic of early stages of de-
velopment. Thus, the association of peripherins with axonal
regeneration, the expression of peripherins in “phylogeneti-
cally old” populations of neurons, and the expression of Xe-
nopus peripherin in embryonic neuroepithelia and a “prim-
itive” type of glial cell are all consistent with the retention of
features inherited from an ancestral IF protein originally
expressed in neurons and glia.
It still remains to be seen whether differences in IF gene
expression and structural properties among orthologous IFs
in fact do contribute to the variations in the capacities for
growth and regeneration that are found among various or-
ganisms. Our results argue that, when considering how and
why IF proteins have evolved, differences in expression must
be considered along with structural variations. The expres-
sion of a Xenopus peripherin in newly differentiating glia
and neurons further argues that, in Xenopus, the structural
and assembly properties of XIF3 must at least be consistent
with requirements for ﬂexibility and support of early process
outgrowth in both kinds of cells. The addition of type IV nIF
proteins in neurons and of other IF proteins, such as GFAP,
in subsets of glia, thus, may serve to modulate the cytoskel-
etal structures of these cells to support more specialized
features as they emerge during development.

===== Ghazal2019_StemCellRep_EC+RNAseq =====
Stem Cell Reports
Resource
RNA Proﬁling of the Human and Mouse Spinal Cord Stem Cell Niches Reveals
an Embryonic-like Regionalization with MSX1+ Roof-Plate-Derived Cells
Hussein Ghazale,1,9 Chantal Ripoll,1,9 Nicolas Leventoux,1 Laurent Jacob,2 Safa Azar,1 Daria Mamaeva,1
Yael Glasson,1 Charles-Felix Calvo,3 Jean-Leon Thomas,2,4 Sarah Meneceur,5 Yvan Lallemand,5
Vale´rie Rigau,1,6 Florence E. Perrin,8 Harun N. Noristani,8 Brenda Rocamonde,2 Emmanuelle Huillard,2
Luc Bauchet,1,7 and Jean-Philippe Hugnot1,8,*
1INSERM U1051, INM, Hopital Saint Eloi, 80 Avenue Augustin Fliche, 34091 Montpellier, France
2Inserm U 1127, CNRS UMR 7225, Sorbonne Universite´s, UPMC Univ Paris 06 UMR S 1127, Institut du Cerveau et de la Moelle e´pinie`re, ICM, 75013, Paris,
France
3Neuroglial Interactions in Cerebral Physiopathology. CIRB, CNRS UMR 7241/INSERM U1050 Colle`ge de France 11, Place Marcelin Berthelot, 75005 Paris,
France
4Department of Neurology, Yale University School of Medicine, New Haven, CT 06511, USA
5Institut Pasteur, Department of Developmental and Stem Cell Biology, UMR3738 CNRS, Paris 75015, France
6CHU of Montpellier, Hopital Gui de Chaulliac, Pathology Department, 80 Avenue Augustin Fliche, 34091 Montpellier, France
7CHU of Montpellier, Hopital Gui de Chaulliac, Neurosurgery Department, 80 Avenue Augustin Fliche, 34091 Montpellier, France
8University of Montpellier, Faculte´ des Sciences, Place Euge`ne Bataillon, 34095 Montpellier Cedex 5, France
9Co-ﬁrst author
*Correspondence: jean-philippe.hugnot@umontpellier.fr
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.stemcr.2019.04.001
SUMMARY
Anamniotes, rodents, and young humans maintain neural stem cells in the ependymal zone (EZ) around the central canal of the spinal
cord, representing a possible endogenous source for repair in mammalian lesions. Cell diversity and genes speciﬁc for this region are ill
deﬁned. A cellular and molecular resource is provided here for the mouse and human EZ based on RNA proﬁling, immunostaining, and
ﬂuorescent transgenic mice. This uncovered the conserved expression of 1,200 genes including 120 transcription factors. Unexpectedly
the EZ maintains an embryonic-like dorsal-ventral pattern of expression of spinal cord developmental transcription factors (ARX, FOXA2,
MSX1, and PAX6). In mice, dorsal and ventral EZ cells express Vegfr3 and are derived from the embryonic roof and ﬂoor plates. The dorsal
EZ expresses a high level of Bmp6 and Gdf10 genes and harbors a subpopulation of radial quiescent cells expressing MSX1 and ID4 tran-
scription factors.
INTRODUCTION
The adult central nervous system maintains neural stem
cells in speciﬁc areas called niches (Gage and Temple,
2013). The main stem cell pools are in the subventricular
zone (SVZ) and in the hippocampus. A third stem cell niche
is found in the adult spinal cord around the central canal in
anamniotes, rodents, and humans (Becker et al., 2018;
Marichal et al., 2017; Stenudd et al., 2015). This niche orig-
inates from the embryonic neuroepithelium and forms the
spinal cord ependymal zone (EZ) organized as a pseudo-
epithelium. As in the brain, this stem cell niche is highly
organized and contains stem and non-stem cells. Four
different cell types, namely ependymocytes, cerebrospi-
nal-ﬂuid-contacting neurons (CSF-N), vessels, and long
radial cells have been described in mice. In particular, the
EZ presents a dorsal-ventral regionalization with long
radial glial ﬁbrillary acidic protein (GFAP)+ cells in the dor-
sal part (Sabourin et al., 2009). In contrast to brain, spinal
cord ependymal cells slowly proliferate to self-renew (Al-
faro-Cervello et al., 2012; Pfenninger et al., 2011). In cul-
tures, a fraction of these cells can also generate passageable
neurospheres (i.e. clonal expansion of neural precursor
cells) which can generate astrocytes, oligodendrocytes,
and neurons after differentiation (Weiss et al., 1996). The
identity of these neurosphere-forming cells in the EZ is still
not completely clear as both GFAP+ and GFAP ependymal
cells can behave as neural stem cells in vitro (Barnabe´-
Heider et al., 2010; Fiorelli et al., 2013; Sabourin et al.,
2009; Xu et al., 2017). Recent single cell analysis has iden-
tiﬁed neurogenesis in the adult spinal cord (Habib et al.,
2016); however, whether these new neurons are derived
from the EZ is not yet established. It has been known since
1962 (Adrian and Walker, 1962) that the EZ can readily acti-
vate and produce new cells upon injury (Becker et al.,
2018). Depending on the lesion type and severity, EZ-
derived cells can signiﬁcantly contribute to the glial scar
formation (Ren et al., 2017; Stenudd et al., 2015).
In comparison with the brain niches, less is known about
the adult spinal cord EZ. Reminiscent of the mouse niche,
in human, ependymal cells around the central canal
display immature features such as expression of NES (nes-
tin), VIM (vimentin), and SOX2 (Becker et al., 2018). How-
ever, with aging the central lumen can disappear and the
EZ is disorganized (Garcia-Ovejero et al., 2015). Multipo-
tent neurospheres with a limited proliferation ability
Stem Cell Reports j Vol. 12 j 1159–1177 j May 14, 2019 j ª 2019 The Author(s).
1159
This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

(legend on next page)
1160
Stem Cell Reports j Vol. 12 j 1159–1177 j May 14, 2019

have been derived from the human spinal cord (Dromard
et al., 2008) and using alternative culture conditions,
Mothe et al. (2011) were able to maintain a sustained pro-
liferation of multipotent human-derived neural stem cells.
A detailed transcriptomic proﬁling of the human and
mouse EZ is currently lacking. This would help us to under-
stand the speciﬁcity and diversity of these cells as well as
identify
gene
expressions
and
molecular
pathways
conserved between primates and rodents. It would also
provide important insights into why, in contrast to anam-
niotes, mammalian ependymal cells cannot regenerate
neurons after spinal cord injury (Becker et al., 2018).
Here we provide a cellular and molecular resource for the
mouse and human EZ based on RNA proﬁling, immuno-
staining, and ﬂuorescent transgenic mice. This uncovered
the conserved expression of 1,200 genes speciﬁcally ex-
pressed in the EZ, including 120 transcription factors
(TFs). Unexpectedly, the EZ maintains an embryonic-like
dorsal-ventral pattern of expression of spinal cord develop-
mental TFs. New subpopulations of cells expressing speciﬁc
genes were identiﬁed in the dorsal and ventral part of the
EZ. In mice, dorsal ependymal cells were found to be
derived from the embryonic spinal cord roof plate.
RESULTS
Genes Enriched in the Adult Human and Mouse EZ
An epithelial organization of the EZ is observed both in hu-
man and mouse as evidenced by CTNNB1 (b-catenin) and
CD24 stainings (Figures 2 and 3). To identify gene expres-
sion enriched in the EZ, we microdissected this region
and adjacent tissue in two human samples and four mice
(Figure 1A). For human, we selected two samples with a
lumen from patients aged 17 and 46 years. Microarrays
were used for RNA proﬁling and heatmaps indicated
adequate clustering of EZ samples (Figure 1B). Volcano
plots showed 8,733 and 2,122 genes enriched (fold
change R2) in the mouse and human EZ, respectively,
and 1,223 genes commonly enriched in both species (Fig-
ures 1C and 1D). Table 1 shows the top 15 genes commonly
enriched in the human and mouse EZ and genes that are
more speciﬁcally enriched in human or mice. Figure S1A
shows examples of identiﬁed genes whose speciﬁc expres-
sion in the mouse EZ is conﬁrmed in the Allen brain atlas
(Lein et al., 2007). In mouse and human, GO (Gene
Ontology) and KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and
Genomes) pathway analyses revealed an enrichment for
genes involved in cilia formation, smoothened and hippo
pathways, cell division, and transcription (Figure 1E and
Tables S1, S2, and S3). In addition, consistent with their
position at the interface between CSF and the nervous
parenchyma, ependymal cells also expressed 129 and 34
members of the solute carrier family in mice and human,
respectively (Tables S1, S2, and S3). Some of them are
very speciﬁcally (fold change >30) expressed in the mouse
EZ such as Slc26A3, Slc14a1, and Slc16a12, which transport
chloride, urea, and monocarboxylic acid, respectively. Un-
expectedly, Cftr, a chloride transporter responsible for
cystic ﬁbrosis, was found to be speciﬁcally expressed in
the EZ in both species (Table 1). Using a single cell tran-
scriptome approach in the mouse spinal cord, two recent
studies (Rosenberg et al., 2018; Zeisel et al., 2018) provided
a limited number of genes (<50) enriched in spinal cord
ependymal cells and CSF-N, and most of them were identi-
ﬁed in our study (Table S4). Interestingly, only Tmem212, a
gene coding for a transmembrane protein with few annota-
tions, was identiﬁed in the three studies (including ours) as
being enriched in the ependymal cells. With regard to
CSF-N, Espn (Espin) and Pkd2l1 were identiﬁed in the three
studies (Table S4).
We and others have reported that the dorsal and ventral
parts of the mouse EZ have distinctive features such as the
presence of radial cells expressing NES protein (Alfaro-Cer-
vello et al., 2012; Becker et al., 2018; Hamilton et al.,
2009) and the preferential expression of the TF ZEB1 in
the dorsal part (Sabourin et al., 2009). Further exploration
of regional gene expression was done by microdissecting
the dorsal, lateral, and ventral parts of the mouse EZ (Fig-
ure 1F, n = 4 mice). Only few regionally-expressed
genes were identiﬁed (Figure 1G and Table S5). In the
ventral part, we found a strong expression of three genes
Figure 1. RNA Proﬁles of the Mouse and Human EZ
(A) Aspect of EZ in the mouse (mEZ, thoraco level) and human samples (hEZ1 and hEZ2, thoracolumbar level). A lumen was present in mice
and in the two human (aged 17 and 46 years) samples. Microdissected EZ and peri EZ regions are delimited with dotted circles. Scale
bars, 100 mm.
(B) Heatmap of hierarchical clustering of genes expressed in EZ and peri EZ (pEZ) regions in the four mouse and two human samples.
(C) Volcano plots of genes whose expression is enriched in the mouse and human EZ (fold change R2).
(D) Venn diagram of genes enriched in the mouse and human EZ.
(E) Genes enriched for ciliogenesis, smoothened pathway (Smo.), division, and transcription factors in the mouse and human EZ.
Expression of genes in red was subsequently conﬁrmed at the protein level (Figures 2 and 3).
(F) Microdissected subregions of the mouse EZ.
(G) Genes enriched (top 9) in the ventral and dorsal EZ (full lists are in Table S5).
